where $\bar{y}_{1}$ and $\bar{y}_{2}$ are the sample means and $S=\left(s_{j k}\right)$ is the sample covariance matrix. By properties of the bivariate normal distribution (e.g., Stuart and Ord 1994, section 7.22), the conditional distribution of $y_{i 2}$ given $y_{i 1}$ is normal with mean $\mu_{2}+\beta_{21 \cdot 1}\left(y_{i 1}-\mu_{1}\right)$ and variance $\sigma_{22 \cdot 1}$, where

$$
\beta_{21 \cdot 1}=\sigma_{12} / \sigma_{11} \text { and } \sigma_{22 \cdot 1}=\sigma_{22}-\sigma_{12}^{2} / \sigma_{11}
$$

are, respectively, the slope and residual variance from the regression of $Y_{2}$ on $Y_{1}$. By Property 6.1, the ML estimates of these quantities are

$$
\hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1}=\hat{\sigma}_{12} / \hat{\sigma}_{11}=s_{12} / s_{11}
$$

the least squares estimate of the slope, and

$$
\hat{\sigma}_{22 \cdot 1}=\hat{\sigma}_{22}-\hat{\sigma}_{12}^{2} / \hat{\sigma}_{11}=\mathrm{RSS} / n
$$

where RSS $=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left\{y_{i 2}-\bar{y}_{2}-\hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1}\left(y_{i 1}-\bar{y}_{1}\right)\right\}^{2}$ is the residual sum of squares from the regression based on the $n$ sampled units.

The ML estimates of $\beta_{21 \cdot 1}$ and $\sigma_{22 \cdot 1}$ can also be derived directly from the likelihood based on the conditional distribution of $Y_{2}$ given $Y_{1}$. The connection between ML estimation for the normal linear regression model and least squares applies more generally, as discussed in the next example.

Example 6.10 Multiple Linear Regression, Unweighted, and Weighted. The data consist of $n$ units $\left(y_{i}, x_{i 1}, \ldots, x_{i p}\right), i=1, \ldots, n$ on an outcome variable $Y$ and $p$ predictor variables $X=\left(X_{1}, \ldots, X_{p}\right)$. Assume that, given the $n$ values of $x_{i}=\left(x_{i 1}, \ldots, x_{i p}\right)$, the values of $y_{i}$ are independent normal random variables with mean $\beta_{0}+\sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_{j} x_{i j}$ and common variance $\sigma^{2}$. The loglikelihood of $\theta=\left(\beta, \sigma^{2}\right)$, where $\beta=\left(\beta_{0}, \beta_{1}, \ldots, \beta_{p}\right)$, given observed data $\left\{\left(x_{i}, y_{i}\right), i=1, \ldots, n\right\}$ is

$$
\ell_{Y}(\theta \mid y)=-(n / 2) \ln \sigma^{2}-\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_{i}-\beta_{0}-\beta_{1} x_{i 1}-\cdots-\beta_{p} x_{i p}\right)^{2} /\left(2 \sigma^{2}\right)
$$

Maximizing this expression with respect to $\theta$, the ML estimates of $\left(\beta_{0}, \ldots, \beta_{p}\right)$ are found to be the least squares estimates of the intercept and the regression coefficients. Specifically, let

$$
X=\left(\begin{array}{cccc}
1 & x_{11} & \cdots & x_{1 p} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
1 & x_{n 1} & \cdots & x_{n p}
\end{array}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad Y=\left(\begin{array}{c}
y_{1} \\
\vdots \\
y_{n}
\end{array}\right)
$$

be the $n \times(p+1)$ matrix of predictors including the constant term and the vector of outcomes, respectively. Then,

$$
\hat{\beta}=\left(X^{\mathrm{T}} X\right)^{-1} X^{\mathrm{T}} Y .
$$

The ML estimate of $\sigma^{2}$ is

$$
\hat{\sigma}^{2}=(Y-X \hat{\beta})^{\mathrm{T}}(Y-X \hat{\beta}) / n \equiv \mathrm{RSS} / n,
$$

where RSS is the residual sum of squares from the least squares regression, the generalization of RSS in Example 6.9. Because the divisor here is $n$ rather than $n-p-1$, the ML estimate of $\sigma^{2}$ again does not correct for the loss of degrees of freedom when estimating the $p+1$ location parameters, ( $\beta_{0}, \beta_{1}, \ldots, \beta_{p}$ ).

A simple but important extension is weighted multiple linear regression. Suppose the mean of $y_{i}$ is still $\mu_{i}=\beta_{0}+\sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_{j} x_{i j}$, but now its variance is $\sigma^{2} / w_{i}$, where $\left\{w_{i}\right\}$ are known positive constants. Thus, $\left(y_{i}-\mu_{i}\right) \sqrt{w_{i}}$ are independent, identically distributed (iid) $N\left(0, \sigma^{2}\right)$, and the log-likelihood is

$$
\ell_{Y}(\theta \mid y)=-(n / 2) \ln \sigma^{2}-\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{i}\left(y_{i}-\mu_{i}\right)^{2} /\left(2 \sigma^{2}\right) .
$$

Maximizing this function yields ML estimates given by the weighted least squares estimates

$$
\hat{\beta}=\left(X^{\mathrm{T}} W X\right)^{-1}\left(X^{\mathrm{T}} W Y\right),
$$

and

$$
\hat{\sigma}^{2}=(Y-X \hat{\beta})^{\mathrm{T}} W(Y-X \hat{\beta}) / n
$$

where $W=\operatorname{Diag}\left(w_{1}, \ldots, w_{n}\right)$.
Example 6.11 Generalized Linear Models. Suppose that the data again consist of $n$ units $\left(y_{i}, x_{i 1}, \ldots, x_{i p}\right), i=1, \ldots, n$ of an outcome variable $Y$ and $p$ predictor variables $X_{1}, \ldots, X_{p}$. A more general class of models assumes that, given
$x_{i}=\left(x_{i 1}, \ldots, x_{i p}\right)$, the $n$ values of $y_{i}$ are an independent sample from a regular exponential family distribution:

$$
f_{Y}\left(y_{i} \mid x_{i}, \beta, \phi\right)=\exp \left[\left(y_{i} \delta\left(x_{i}, \beta\right)-b\left(\delta\left(x_{i}, \beta\right)\right)\right) / \phi+c\left(y_{i}, \phi\right)\right]
$$

where $\delta(\cdot, \cdot)$ and $b(\cdot)$ are known functions that determine the distribution of $y_{i}$, and $c\left(y_{i}, \phi\right)$ is a known function indexed by a scale parameter $\phi$. The mean of $y_{i}$ is assumed related to the covariates $x_{i}$ by the expression:

$$
E\left(y_{i} \mid x_{i}, \beta, \phi\right)=g^{-1}\left(\beta_{0}+\sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_{j} x_{i j}\right)
$$

or

$$
g\left(\mu_{i}\right)=\beta_{0}+\sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_{j} x_{i j}
$$

where $\mu_{i}=E\left(y_{i} \mid x_{i}, \beta, \phi\right)$, and $g(\cdot)$ is a known one-to-one function. The function $g(\cdot)$ is called the link function because it "links" the expectation of $y_{i}, \mu_{i}$, to a linear combination of the covariates. The canonical link $g_{c}$ is that for which

$$
g_{c}\left(\mu_{i}\right)=\delta\left(x_{i}, \beta\right)=\beta_{0}+\sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_{j} x_{i j}
$$

and is obtained by setting $g(\cdot)$ equal to the inverse of the derivative of $b(\cdot)$ with respect to its argument (see Problems 6.6 and 6.7 for more details). This choice of link function is a natural starting point for modeling many data sets. Important specific models with their canonical links include the following:
Normal linear regression: $g_{c}=$ identity, $b(\delta)=\delta^{2} / 2, \phi=\sigma^{2}$;
Poisson regression: $g_{c}=\log , b(\delta)=\exp (\delta), \phi=1$; and
Logistic regression: $g_{c}=\operatorname{logit}$, where $\operatorname{logit}\left(\mu_{i}\right)=\log \left(\mu_{i} /\left(1-\mu_{i}\right)\right), b(\delta)= \log (1+\exp (\delta)), \phi=1$.

The loglikelihood of $\theta=(\beta, \phi)$ given observed data $\left(y_{i}, x_{i}\right), i=1, \ldots, n$ based on Eq. (6.9) is

$$
\ell_{Y}(\theta \mid y)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[\left(y_{i} \delta\left(x_{i}, \beta\right)-b\left(\delta\left(x_{i}, \beta\right)\right)\right) / \phi+c\left(y_{i}, \phi\right)\right]
$$

which for nonnormal cases does not generally have an explicit maximum. Numerical maximization can be achieved using an iterative algorithm, such as the Fisher scoring algorithm (McCullagh and Nelder 1989, section 2.5; Firth 1991, section 3.4).

Example 6.12 Normal Repeated Measures Models. In longitudinal studies, units are observed at different times and/or under different experimental conditions. The following general repeated measures model is given in Jennrich and Schluchter (1986) and builds on earlier work by Hartley and Rao (1967), Harville (1977), Laird and Ware (1982), and Ware (1985). Suppose that the complete data for unit $i$ consist of $K$ measurements $y_{i}=\left(y_{i 1}, \ldots, y_{i K}\right)$ of an outcome variable $Y$ and that $y_{i}$ are independent with distribution

$$
y_{i} \sim N_{K}\left(X_{i} \beta, \Sigma(\psi)\right),
$$

where $X_{i}$ is a known ( $K \times m$ ) design matrix for unit $i, \beta$ is a ( $m \times 1$ ) vector of unknown regression coefficients, and the elements of the covariance matrix $\Sigma$ are known functions of $q$ unknown parameters $\psi$. The model thus incorporates a mean structure, defined by $\beta$ and the set of design matrices $\left\{X_{i}\right\}$, and a covariance structure defined by the form of the covariance matrix $\Sigma$. The observed data consist of the design matrices $\left\{X_{i}\right\}$ and outcome measurements $\left\{y_{i}, i=1, \ldots, n\right\}$.

A large number of situations can be modeled by combining different choices of mean and covariance structures. Common covariance structures include:

Independence: $\Sigma=\operatorname{Diag}_{K}\left(\psi_{1}, \ldots, \psi_{K}\right)$, a diagonal ( $K \times K$ ) matrix,
Compound symmetry: $\Sigma=\psi_{1} U_{K}+\psi_{2} I_{K}, \psi_{1}$ and $\psi_{2}$ scalar, $U_{K}=(K \times K)$ matrix of ones, $I_{K}=(K \times K)$ identity matrix,
Autoregressive, lag 1: $\Sigma=\left(\sigma_{j k}\right), \sigma_{j k}=\psi_{1} \psi_{2}^{|j-k|}, \psi_{1}, \psi_{2}$ scalars,
Banded: $\Sigma=\left(\sigma_{j k}\right), \sigma_{j k}=\psi_{r}$, where $r=|j-k|+1, r=1, \ldots, K$,
Factor analytic: $\Sigma=\Gamma \Gamma^{\mathrm{T}}+\psi_{0}$, with $\Gamma=(K \times q)$ the matrix of "factor loadings," $\psi_{0}=(K \times K)$ the diagonal matrix of "specific variances," and $\psi=\left(\Gamma, \psi_{0}\right)$.
Random effects: $\Sigma=Z \psi^{*} Z^{\mathrm{T}}+\sigma^{2} I_{K}$, where $Z$ is a ( $K \times q$ ) known matrix, $\psi^{*}$ is a ( $q \times q$ ) dispersion matrix, $\sigma^{2}$ is a scalar, $I_{K}$ the $K \times K$ identity matrix, and $\psi=\left(\psi^{*}, \sigma^{2}\right)$.
Unstructured: $\Sigma=\left(\sigma_{j k}\right)$, with $\psi$ representing the $\nu=K(K+1) / 2$ elements of this matrix.

The mean structure is also flexible. If $X_{i}=I_{K}$, the ( $K \times K$ ) identity matrix, then $\mu_{i}=\beta^{\mathrm{T}}$ for all $i$. Between-subject and within-subject effects are readily modeled through other choices of $X_{i}$.

The loglikelihood for the model (6.13) is

$$
\ell_{Y}(\beta, \psi)=-0.5 n \log |\Sigma(\psi)|-0.5 \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_{i}-X_{i} \beta\right)^{\mathrm{T}} \Sigma^{-1}(\psi)\left(y_{i}-X_{i} \beta\right),
$$

which is linear in the observed quantities $\left\{y_{i}, y_{i}^{\mathrm{T}} y_{i}, i=1, \ldots, n\right\}$. This likelihood does not yield explicit ML estimates, except in simple special cases. For the
unstructured covariance structure $\Sigma(\psi)=\Sigma$, there is no explicit ML estimate for $(\beta, \Sigma)$, but explicit ML estimates are available for $\beta$ given $\Sigma$ and for $\Sigma$ given $\beta$. Hence, an iterative ML solution is obtained by the following alternating conditional modes algorithm: Given estimates $\left(\beta^{(t)}, \psi^{(t)}\right)$ at iteration $t$, estimates at iteration $t+1$ are

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta^{(t+1)} & =\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(X_{i}^{\mathrm{T}}\left(\Sigma^{(t)}\right)^{-1} X_{i}\right)^{-1} X_{i}^{\mathrm{T}}\left(\Sigma^{(t)}\right)^{-1} y_{i}, \quad \text { and } \\
\Sigma^{(t+1)} & =\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_{i}-X_{i}^{\mathrm{T}} \beta^{(t+1)}\right)^{\mathrm{T}}\left(y_{i}-X_{i}^{\mathrm{T}} \beta^{(t+1)}\right) .
\end{aligned}
$$

For other covariance structures, (6.15) remains unchanged, and (6.16) is replaced by an update for $\psi$ that depends on the specific form of the structure.

A useful variant of ML is restricted ML, which yields estimates that correct for the loss of degrees of freedom from estimating location parameters in normal models, a correction that is not made with ML estimates, as noted in Examples 6.7, 6.8, and 6.10. Restricted ML was originally formulated for analysis of variance (ANOVA) models and linear mixed models (see, for example, Harville 1977) as the likelihood corresponding to the largest set of independent error contrasts of the data, where a contrast is a linear combination of the data with mean zero. More generally, it can be viewed as maximizing the function obtained by including a Bayesian prior distribution for the location parameters $\beta$ and then integrating these parameters out of the product of the prior distribution and the likelihood function before maximizing the resulting function of $\psi$.

Other modifications of maximum likelihood, including conditional likelihood, marginal likelihood, or partial likelihood, maximize pieces of the likelihood rather than the full likelihood, generally to eliminate nuisance parameters and thereby simplify the problem. In the missing data context, this strategy can be applied to avoid modeling the missingness mechanism in certain situations, at the expense of some loss of information.

### 6.1.2 Inference Based on the Likelihood

By ML inference, we mean the ML estimate and a method for assessing its uncertainty, via hypothesis tests, confidence intervals, or Bayesian posterior distributions. We distinguish between (i) "pure" or "direct" likelihood inference (Edwards 1992; Royall 1997; Frumento et al. 2016), which involves the data solely through likelihood ratios $L_{Y}(y \mid \theta) / L_{Y}\left(y \mid \theta^{*}\right)$ for pairs of parameter values $\theta, \theta^{*}$, with data $y$ fixed at its observed value and (ii) frequentist likelihood inference, meaning tests, or confidence intervals based on the repeated sampling distribution of the ML estimate.

The most popular form of direct likelihood inference is Bayesian inference, where the parameter $\theta$ is assigned a prior distribution $p(\theta)$, and inference about



$\theta$, after observing the data $y$, is based on its posterior distribution $p(\theta \mid y)$, determined by Bayes' theorem:

$$
p(\theta \mid y)=\frac{p(\theta) L_{Y}(y \mid \theta)}{p(y)},
$$

where $p(y)=\int p(\theta) L_{Y}(y \mid \theta) \mathrm{d} \theta$ is the normalizing constant.
Point estimates of $\theta$ can be obtained as measures of the "center" of the posterior distribution, such as the posterior mean, median, or mode. In a Bayesian analysis, which always includes a prior distribution for $\theta$ in the model specification, we denote the mode of the posterior distribution $p(\theta \mid y)$ by $\hat{\theta}$. It corresponds to the ML estimate when the prior distribution is uniform:

$$
p(\theta)=\text { const. for all possible } \theta .
$$

The latter is not a real probability distribution unless the parameter space has compact support, but it can be used to approximate the lack of prior knowledge about $\theta$, providing care is taken to ensure that the resulting posterior distribution is well defined.

There are strong parallels between Bayesian and frequentist likelihood inference in the case of large samples, to which we now turn.

### 6.1.3 Large Sample Maximum Likelihood and Bayes Inference

In this section, we outline some basic large-sample properties of frequentist ML and Bayesian inference. References to these results include Huber (1967), DeGroot (1970), Rao (1972), Cox and Hinkley (1974), White (1982), and Gelman et al. (2013).

Let $\hat{\theta}$ denote an ML estimate of $\theta$ based on observed data $y$, or the posterior mode in a Bayesian analysis, and suppose that the model is correctly specified. The most important practical property of $\hat{\theta}$ is that, in many cases, especially with large samples, the following approximation is applicable.

## Approximation 6.1

$$
(\theta-\hat{\theta}) \sim N(0, C),
$$

where $C$ is an estimate of the $d \times d$ covariance matrix for $(\theta-\hat{\theta}) .^{1}$

This approximation has both a frequentist and a Bayesian interpretation. The Bayesian version of Approximation 6.1 treats $\theta$ as the random variable and $\hat{\theta}$ as the mode of the posterior distribution, fixed by the observed data. The interpretation of (6.18) is then that given $f(\cdot \mid \cdot)$ and conditional on the observed values of the data, the posterior distribution of $\theta$ is normal with mean $\hat{\theta}$ and covariance
matrix $C$, where $\hat{\theta}$ and $C$ are statistics fixed at their observed values. The theoretical justification is based on a Taylor series expansion of the loglikelihood about the ML estimate, namely,

$$
\ell_{Y}(\theta \mid y)=\ell_{Y}(\hat{\theta} \mid y)+(\theta-\hat{\theta})^{\mathrm{T}} D_{\ell}(\hat{\theta} \mid y)-\frac{1}{2}(\theta-\hat{\theta})^{\mathrm{T}} I_{Y}(\hat{\theta} \mid y)(\theta-\hat{\theta})+r(\theta \mid y)
$$

where $D_{\ell}(\theta \mid y)$ is the score function, and $I_{Y}(\theta \mid y)$ is the observed information:

$$
I_{Y}(\theta \mid y)=-\frac{\partial^{2} \ell_{Y}(\theta \mid y)}{\partial \theta \partial \theta}
$$

By definition, $D_{\ell}(\hat{\theta} \mid y)=0$. Hence, provided the remainder term $r(\theta \mid y)$ can be neglected, and the prior distribution of $\theta$ is relatively flat in the range of $\theta$ supported by the data, the posterior distribution of $\theta$ has density

$$
f_{Y}(\theta \mid y) \propto \exp \left[-\frac{1}{2}(\theta-\hat{\theta})^{\mathrm{T}} I_{Y}(\hat{\theta} \mid y)(\theta-\hat{\theta})\right]
$$

which is the normal distribution of Approximation 6.1 with covariance matrix

$$
C=I_{Y}^{-1}(\hat{\theta} \mid y)
$$

the inverse of the observed information evaluated at $\hat{\theta}$.
Technical regularity conditions for these results are discussed in the cited texts. Gelman et al. (2013) describe the following situations where the results can be expected to fail:

S1: "Underidentified" models, where the information on one or more parameters in the likelihood does not increase with the sample size.
S2: Models where the number of parameters increases with the sample size. We assume that the number of components of $\theta$ does not increase with the sample size, and hence do not consider "nonparametric" or "semiparametric" models where the number of parameters increases with the sample size. Asymptotic results for such models are tricky and require that the rate of increase of the number of parameters be carefully controlled.
S3: Unbounded likelihoods, where there is no ML estimate or posterior mode in the interior of the parameter space. This problem can often be avoided by not allowing isolated poles of the likelihood function, for example, by bounding variance parameters away from zero.
S4: Improper posterior distributions, which arise in some settings when improper prior distributions are specified. See, for example, Gelman et al. (2013).

S5: Points of convergence on the edge of the parameter space or with no support from the prior distribution. These problems can be avoided by checking the
plausibility of the model and giving positive prior probability to all values of the parameter, even those that are remotely plausible.
S6: Slow convergence in the tails of the distribution. The normal limiting distribution in Approximation 6.1 implies exponentially decaying tails, and this property may not be achieved quickly enough to provide valid inferences for realistic sample sizes. In such cases, a better approach may be to avoid the asymptotic approximation and concentrate on estimating the posterior distribution for a reasonable choice (or choices) of prior distributions. Alternatively, a transformation of the parameters may improve Approximation 6.1. This motivates the following.

Property 6.2 Let $g(\theta)$ be a monotone differentiable function of $\theta$, and let $C$ be the estimated large sample covariance matrix of $\theta-\hat{\theta}$, as in Approximation 6.1. Then, an estimate of the large-sample covariance matrix of $g(\theta)-g(\hat{\theta})$ is

$$
D_{g}(\hat{\theta}) \subset D_{g}(\hat{\theta})^{\mathrm{T}},
$$

where $D_{g}(\theta)=\partial g(\theta) / \partial \theta$ is the partial derivative of $g$ with respect to $\theta$. Property 6.2 follows from the first term of a Taylor series expansion of $g(\theta)$ about $\theta=\hat{\theta}$ and leads to the following approximation.

## Approximation 6.2

$$
g(\theta)-g(\hat{\theta}) \sim N\left[0, \quad D_{g}(\hat{\theta}) \subset D_{g}(\hat{\theta})^{\mathrm{T}}\right] .
$$

When sample sizes are not large, it is often useful to find a transformation $g(\cdot)$ that makes the normality in Approximation 6.2 more accurate than in Approximation 6.1, for example, using $\ln \left(\sigma^{2}\right)$ instead of $\sigma^{2}$ in Example 6.7 or Fisher's normalizing transformation $Z_{\rho}=\ln ((1+\rho) /(1-\rho)) / 2$ instead of the correlation $\rho=\sigma_{12} / \sqrt{\sigma_{11} \sigma_{22}}$ in Example 6.9.

The frequentist interpretation of Approximation 6.1 is that under $f(\cdot \mid \cdot)$ in repeated samples with fixed $\theta, \hat{\theta}$ will be approximately normally distributed with mean equal to the true value of $\theta$ and covariance matrix $C$, which has lower-order variability than $\hat{\theta}$. The theoretical justification first approximates $D_{\ell}(\hat{\theta} \mid y)$ about the true value of $\theta$ by the first term of a Taylor series:

$$
0=D_{\ell}(\hat{\theta} \mid y)=D_{\ell}(\theta \mid y)-I_{Y}(\theta \mid y)(\hat{\theta}-\theta)+r(\hat{\theta} \mid y) .
$$

If the remainder term $r(\hat{\theta} \mid y)$ is negligible, we have

$$
D_{\ell}(\theta \mid y) \approx I_{Y}(\theta \mid y)(\hat{\theta}-\theta) .
$$

Under certain regularity conditions, it can be shown by a central limit theorem that in repeated sampling, $D_{\ell}(\theta \mid y)$ is asymptotically normal with mean 0 and covariance matrix

$$
J_{Y}(\theta)=E\left(I_{Y}(\theta \mid y) \mid \theta\right)=\int I_{Y}(\theta \mid y) f_{Y}(y \mid \theta) \mathrm{d} y
$$

which is called the expected information matrix. A version of the law of large numbers implies that

$$
J_{Y}(\theta) \cong J_{Y}(\hat{\theta}) \cong I_{Y}(\hat{\theta} \mid y)
$$

Combining these facts leads to Approximation 6.1, with covariance matrix

$$
C=J_{Y}^{-1}(\hat{\theta})
$$

the inverse of the expected information evaluated at $\theta=\hat{\theta}$, or

$$
C=I_{Y}^{-1}(\hat{\theta} \mid y)
$$

the inverse of the observed information evaluated at $\theta=\hat{\theta}$. Ancillarity arguments (Efron and Hinkley 1978) suggest that the observed information $I_{Y}^{-1}(\hat{\theta} \mid y)$ provides a better estimate of precision than the expected information $J_{Y}^{-1}(\hat{\theta})$. If a transformation of $\theta$ is applied to improve normality, these choices of $C$ can also be substituted in Eq. (6.15) to obtain a frequentist version of Approximation 6.2.

Approximations 6.1 and 6.2 are based on the assumption that the model is correctly specified, that is, that $Y$ is sampled from the $\operatorname{density} f_{Y}\left(y \mid \theta_{0}\right)$ for some true value $\theta_{0}$ of the parameter. If the model is misspecified, and $Y$ is in fact sampled from the true density $f_{Y}^{*}(Y)$, the posterior mode or the ML estimate converges to the value $\theta^{*}$ of $\theta$ that maximizes the Kullback-Liebler Information $E\left[\log \left(f_{Y}(y \mid \theta) / f_{Y}^{*}(y)\right)\right]$ of the model distribution $f_{Y}(y \mid \theta)$ with respect to the true distribution $f_{Y}^{*}(y)$. The frequentist version of Approximation 6.1 can then be replaced by:

## Approximation 6.3

$$
\left(\hat{\theta} \mid f^{*}\right) \sim N\left(\theta^{*}, C^{*}\right)
$$

where $\theta^{*}$ is previously defined and

$$
C^{*}=J_{Y}^{-1}(\theta) K_{Y}(\theta) J_{Y}^{-1}(\theta)
$$

where $K_{Y}(\theta)=E\left(D_{\ell}(\theta) D_{\ell}(\theta)^{\mathrm{T}}\right)$. When the model is correctly specified, $\theta^{*}$ equals the true value of $\theta$, namely $\theta_{0}$, and $C^{*}$ reduces to $J^{-1}\left(\theta_{0}\right)$ (White 1982). In situations where the model is incorrectly specified, but $\theta^{*}$ is nevertheless the parameter of interest, as when $\hat{\theta}$ is consistent for $\theta$, the covariance matrix of $\hat{\theta}$ can then be consistently estimated by the so-called sandwich estimator of $C^{*}$ :

$$
\hat{C}^{*}=I_{Y}^{-1}(\hat{\theta} \mid y) \hat{K}_{Y}(\hat{\theta}) I_{Y}^{-1}(\hat{\theta} \mid y), \quad \text { where } \hat{K}_{Y}(\hat{\theta})=D_{\ell}(\hat{\theta}) D_{\ell}(\hat{\theta})^{\mathrm{T}} .
$$

This estimator is less precise than the observed or expected information but provides some protection against model misspecification. See Gelman et al. (2013) for more details. For a Bayesian interpretation of the sandwich estimator, see Szpiro et al. (2010).

Another method for calculating variances takes bootstrap samples of the data and calculates the ML estimates on each. The sample variance of these bootstrap estimates is asymptotically equivalent to the variance computed using (6.21). The jackknife can also be used to provide asymptotic standard errors. An introduction to these methods is given in Section 5.3. For more discussion, see Efron and Tibshirani (1993) and Miller (1974).

From the Bayesian or frequentist perspectives, Approximations 6.1 or 6.2, with $C$ fixed at $I_{Y}^{-1}(\hat{\theta}), J_{Y}^{-1}(\hat{\theta})$, the sandwich estimator (6.21), or some other approximation, can be used to provide interval estimates for $\theta$. For example, $95 \%$ intervals for scalar $\theta$ are given by

$$
\hat{\theta} \pm 1.96 C^{1 / 2}
$$

where 1.96 can often be replaced by 2 in practice. For vector $\theta, 95 \%$ ellipsoids are given by the inequality

$$
(\theta-\hat{\theta})^{\mathrm{T}} C^{-1}(\theta-\hat{\theta}) \leq \chi_{0.95, d}^{2}
$$

where $\chi_{0.95, d}^{2}$ is the 95th percentile of the chi-squared distribution with degrees of freedom $d$, the dimensionality of $\theta$. More generally, $95 \%$ confidence ellipsoids for $q<d$ components of $\theta$, say $\theta_{(1)}$, can be calculated as

$$
\left(\theta_{(1)}-\hat{\theta}_{(1)}\right)^{\mathrm{T}} C_{(11)}^{-1}\left(\theta_{(1)}-\hat{\theta}_{(1)}\right) \leq \chi_{0.95, q}^{2}
$$

where $\hat{\theta}_{(1)}$ is the ML estimate of $\theta_{(1)}$, and $C_{(11)}$ is the submatrix of $C$ corresponding to $\theta_{(1)}$.

Under $f_{Y}(\cdot \mid \cdot)$ and assuming large enough samples to make Approximation 6.1 appropriate, inference based on Eq. (6.18) is not only appropriate but also asymptotically optimal. It is thus not surprising that the ML estimator for $\theta$ with Approximation 6.1 constitutes a popular applied approach, especially considering that maximizing functions is a highly developed enterprise in many branches of applied mathematics.

Example 6.13 Exponential Sample (Example 6.2 Continued). Differentiating (6.2) twice with respect to $\theta$ gives

$$
I_{Y}(\theta \mid y)=-n / \theta^{2}+2 \sum y_{i} / \theta^{3} .
$$

Taking expectations over $Y$ under the exponential specification for $y_{i}$ gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_{Y}(\theta) & =-n / \theta^{2}+2 E\left(\sum y_{i} \mid \theta\right) / \theta^{3} \\
& =-n / \theta^{2}+2 n \theta / \theta^{3}=n / \theta^{2} .
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting the ML estimate $\hat{\theta}=\bar{y}$ for $\theta$ gives

$$
I_{Y}(\hat{\theta} \mid y)=J_{Y}(\hat{\theta})=n / \bar{y}^{2}
$$

whence the large sample variance of $\theta-\hat{\theta}$ is estimated as $\bar{y}^{2} / n$.
Example 6.14 Univariate Normal Sample (Example 6.1 Continued). For the univariate normal model of Example 6.1, the asymptotic approximation (6.18) is more appropriately applied to ( $\mu, \log \sigma^{2}$ ) than to ( $\mu, \sigma^{2}$ ). Differentiating (6.1) twice with respect to $\mu$ and $\log \sigma^{2}$ and substituting ML estimates of the parameters yields

$$
I_{Y}\left(\hat{\mu}, \log \hat{\sigma}^{2} \mid y\right)=J_{Y}\left(\hat{\mu}, \log \hat{\sigma}^{2}\right)=\left[\begin{array}{cc}
n / \hat{\sigma}^{2} & 0 \\
0 & n / 2
\end{array}\right] .
$$

Inverting this matrix yields the large sample second moments $\operatorname{Var}(\mu-\hat{\mu})= \hat{\sigma}^{2} / n, \operatorname{Cov}\left(\mu-\hat{\mu}, \log \sigma^{2}-\log \hat{\sigma}^{2}\right)=0$, and $\operatorname{Var}\left(\log \sigma^{2}-\log \hat{\sigma}^{2}\right)=2 / n$, where from Example 6.7, $\hat{\mu}=\bar{y}$ and $\hat{\sigma}^{2}=(n-1) s^{2} / n$.

It is common to summarize evidence about the likely values of a multicomponent $\theta$ by "significance levels" rather than by ellipsoids such as (6.19), particularly when the number of components in $\theta, d$, is greater than two. Specifically, for a null value $\theta_{0}$ of $\theta$, the distance from $\hat{\theta}$ to $\theta_{0}$ can be calculated as the Wald statistic

$$
W\left(\theta_{0}, \hat{\theta}\right)=\left(\theta_{0}-\hat{\theta}\right)^{\mathrm{T}} C^{-1}\left(\theta_{0}-\hat{\theta}\right),
$$

which is the left side of (6.23) evaluated at $\theta=\theta_{0}$. The associated percentile of the chi-squared distribution on $d$ degrees of freedom is the significance level or $p$-value of the null value $\theta_{0}$ :

$$
p_{C}=\operatorname{Pr}\left\{\chi_{d}^{2}>W\left(\theta_{0}, \hat{\theta}\right) \mid \theta=\theta_{0}\right\}
$$

which under Approximation 6.1 does not depend on $\theta_{0}$. From the frequentist perspective, the significance level provides the probability that in repeated sampling with $\theta=\theta_{0}$, the ML estimate will be at least as far from $\theta_{0}$ as the observed

ML estimate $\hat{\theta}$. A size $\alpha$ (two-sided) test of the null hypothesis $H_{0}: \theta=\theta_{0}$ is obtained by rejecting $H_{0}$ when the $p$-value $p_{C}$ is smaller than $\alpha$; common values of $\alpha$ are $0.1,0.05$, and 0.01 .

From the Bayesian perspective, $p_{C}$ gives the large sample posterior probability of the set of $\theta$ values with lower posterior density than $\theta_{0}: \operatorname{Pr}\left[\theta \in\left\{\theta \mid f_{Y}(\theta \mid y)<f_{Y}\left(\theta_{0} \mid y\right)\right\} \mid y\right]$; see Box and Tiao (1973) and Rubin (2004, section 2.10) for discussion and examples.

Under Approximation 6.1, an asymptotically equivalent procedure for calculating significance levels is to use the likelihood ratio (LR) statistic to measure the distance between $\hat{\theta}$ and $\theta_{0}$; this yields

$$
p_{L}=\operatorname{Pr}\left\{\chi_{d}^{2}>\operatorname{LR}\left(\theta_{0}, \hat{\theta}\right)\right\}
$$

where

$$
\operatorname{LR}\left(\theta_{0}, \hat{\theta}\right)=2 \ln \left[L_{Y}(\hat{\theta} \mid y) / L_{Y}\left(\theta_{0} \mid y\right)\right]=2\left[\ell_{Y}(\hat{\theta} \mid y)-\ell_{Y}\left(\theta_{0} \mid y\right)\right] .
$$

More generally, suppose $\theta=\left(\theta_{(1)}, \theta_{(2)}\right)$, and we are interested in evaluating the propriety of a null value of $\theta_{(1)}, \theta_{(1) 0}$, where the number of components in $\theta_{(1)}$ is $q$. This situation commonly arises when comparing the fit of two models A and B , which are termed nested because the parameter space for model B is obtained from that for model A by setting $\theta_{(1)}$ to zero. Two asymptotically equivalent approaches derive significance levels corresponding to $p_{C}$ and $p_{L}$ as follows:

$$
p_{C}\left(\theta_{(1) 0}\right)=\operatorname{Pr}\left\{\chi_{q}^{2}>\left(\theta_{(1) 0}-\hat{\theta}_{(1)}\right)^{\mathrm{T}} C_{(11)}^{-1}\left(\theta_{(1) 0}-\hat{\theta}_{(1)}\right)\right\}
$$

where $C_{(11)}$ is the variance-covariance matrix of $\theta_{(1)}$ as in (6.24), and

$$
p_{L}\left(\theta_{(1) 0}\right)=\operatorname{Pr}\left\{\chi_{q}^{2}>\operatorname{LR}(\hat{\theta}, \tilde{\theta})\right\}
$$

where

$$
\operatorname{LR}(\hat{\theta}, \tilde{\theta})=2\left[\ell_{Y}(\hat{\theta} \mid y)-\ell_{Y}(\tilde{\theta} \mid y)\right]
$$

and $\tilde{\theta}$ is the value of $\theta$ that maximizes $\ell_{Y}(\theta \mid y)$ subject to the constraint that $\theta_{(1)}=\theta_{(1) 0}$. Level $\alpha$ hypothesis tests reject $H_{0}: \theta_{(1)}=\theta_{(1) 0}$, if the $p$-value for $\theta_{(1) 0}$ is smaller than $\alpha$.

Example 6.15 Univariate Normal Sample (Example 6.1 Continued). Suppose $\theta=\left(\mu, \sigma^{2}\right), \theta_{(1)}=\mu$, and $\theta_{(2)}=\sigma^{2}$. To test $H_{0}: \mu=\mu_{0}$, the likelihood ratio test statistic is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{LR} & =2\left(-(n / 2) \ln \left\{(n-1) s^{2} / n\right\}-n / 2+(n / 2) \ln s_{0}^{2}+n / 2\right) \\
& =n \ln \left(n s_{0}^{2} /\left((n-1) s^{2}\right)\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $s_{0}^{2}=n^{-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_{i}-\mu_{0}\right)^{2}=(n-1) s^{2} / n+\left(\bar{y}-\mu_{0}\right)^{2}$. Hence, $\mathrm{LR}=n \ln (1+ \left.t^{2} / n\right)$, where $t^{2}=n^{2}\left(\bar{y}-\mu_{0}\right)^{2} /\left\{(n-1) s^{2}\right\}$ is, from Example 6.14, the test statistic for $H_{0}$ based on the asymptotic variance of $\left(\mu-\mu_{0}\right)$. Asymptotically, LR $=t^{2}$ and is chi-squared distributed with $q=1$ degrees of freedom under $H_{0}$. An exact test is obtained in this case by comparing the statistic $t^{2}$ directly with an $F$ distribution on 1 and $n-1$ degrees of freedom. Such exact small sample tests are rarely available when we apply the likelihood ratio method to data sets with missing values.

Despite the popularity of the approximations discussed here based on asymptotic normality, they can be quite poor in many models with large numbers of parameters or with latent structures. See, for example Rubin and Thayer (1983) and Frumento et al. (2016), which compare asymptotically equivalent approximations and show that the answers can be dramatically different, which can only happen if asymptotic normality fails. Careful Bayesian modeling can yield better results in these settings because the Bayesian approach does not rely on such asymptotic approximations.

### 6.1.4 Bayes Inference Based on the Full Posterior Distribution

The theory of the previous section assumes large samples, which can yield unsatisfactory inferences when the sample size is too small. One approach to this limitation is to adopt a Bayesian perspective and base inferences on the posterior distribution for a particular choice of prior distribution. To measure uncertainty about the point estimate, the posterior standard deviation is analogous to the frequentist sampling standard deviation of the estimate, and posterior probability intervals (such as the 2.5th to 97.5th percentile of the posterior distribution or the $95 \%$ probability interval containing the highest values of the posterior density) are analogous to the frequentist confidence interval. Similarly, the probability associated with the set of values of $\theta$ with lower posterior probability than a null value $\theta_{0}$ replaces the frequentist $p$-value for testing a null hypothesis.

One issue with this approach is that Bayesian inferences with small samples are more sensitive to the choice of prior distribution than inferences with large samples; frequentist statisticians commonly regard this as the Achilles' heel of the Bayesian approach. However, in cases where prior information is available, better inferences, from a frequentist perspective, can result from formally incorporating this information, using the Bayesian machinery. In situations where prior knowledge is more limited, or where "objective" inferences are sought that are not strongly influenced by prior information, the Bayesian approach with dispersed priors often yields inferences that are better from a frequentist perspective than the large-sample approximations of Section 6.1.3.

In particular, the next two examples show that in problems involving the normal distribution, the Bayesian approach with "noninformative" priors can recover the degrees of freedom corrections and Student $t$ reference distribution arising in small-sample frequentist inference.

For more complex problems, including those involving missing data, the Bayesian approach is attractive because it provides answers in situations where no exact frequentist solutions are available. The Bayesian approach is prescriptive, and no matter how complex the problem, any Bayesian answer, once derived, can be evaluated from a frequentist, repeated sampling, perspective. In particular, the method of multiple imputation discussed in Chapter 10 is based on Bayesian principles, and in general, multiple imputation under a realistic model has excellent frequentist properties.

Example 6.16 Bayes Inference for a Univariate Normal Sample with Conjugate Prior (Example 6.1 Continued). For a univariate normal sample with $\theta=\left(\mu, \sigma^{2}\right)$, suppose the following conjugate prior distribution is chosen:

$$
p\left(\mu, \sigma^{2}\right)=p\left(\sigma^{2}\right) p\left(\mu \mid \sigma^{2}\right),
$$

where

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^{2} /\left(v_{0} \sigma_{0}^{2}\right) & \sim \operatorname{Inv}-\chi^{2}\left(v_{0}\right), \\
\left(\mu \mid \sigma^{2}\right) & \sim N\left(\mu_{0}, \sigma^{2} / \kappa_{0}\right),
\end{aligned}
$$

for known $v_{0}, \sigma_{0}^{2}, \mu$, and $\kappa_{0}$. Here, Inv- $\chi^{2}\left(v_{0}\right)$ denotes the inverse of a chisquared distribution with $v_{0}$ degrees of freedom (see Gelman et al. 2013, Appendix A). The prior distribution for $\mu$ given $\sigma^{2}$ is normal. The joint prior density for ( $\mu, \sigma^{2}$ ) thus has the form:

$$
p\left(\mu, \sigma^{2}\right) \propto \sigma^{-1}\left(\sigma^{2}\right)^{-\left(v_{0} / 2+1\right)} \exp \left(-\frac{1}{2 \sigma^{2}}\left[v_{0} \sigma_{0}^{2}+\kappa_{0}\left(\mu_{0}-\mu\right)^{2}\right]\right) .
$$

It can be shown (Gelman et al. 2013, Section 3.3) that the posterior distribution of $\theta$ from data $y=\left(y_{1}, \ldots, y_{n}\right)$ is then

$$
p\left(\mu, \sigma^{2} \mid y\right)=p\left(\sigma^{2} \mid y\right) p\left(\mu \mid \sigma^{2}, y\right),
$$

where the posterior distribution for $\sigma^{2}$ has the scaled inverse chi-squared distribution

$$
\begin{aligned}
{\left[\left(\sigma^{2} / v_{n} \sigma_{n}^{2}\right) \mid y\right] } & \sim \operatorname{Inv}-\chi^{2}\left(v_{n}\right) \\
v_{n} & =v_{0}+n \\
v_{n} \sigma_{n}^{2} & =v_{0} \sigma_{0}^{2}+(n-1) s^{2}+\frac{\kappa_{0} n}{\kappa_{0}+n}\left(\bar{y}-\mu_{0}\right)^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

and the posterior distribution for $\mu$ given $\sigma^{2}$ has the normal distribution

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\mu \mid \sigma^{2}, y\right) & \sim N\left(\mu_{n}, \sigma^{2} / \kappa_{n}\right), \\
\kappa_{n} & =\kappa_{0}+n, \\
\mu_{n} & =\frac{\kappa_{0}}{\kappa_{0}+n} \mu_{0}+\frac{n}{\kappa_{0}+n} \bar{y} .
\end{aligned}
$$

Integrating this conditional posterior distribution over the posterior distribution of $\sigma^{2}$ yields the marginal posterior distribution of $\mu$ :

$$
(\mu \mid y) \sim t\left(\mu_{n}, \sigma_{n}^{2} / \kappa_{n}, v_{n}\right),
$$

the Student's $t$ distribution with center $\mu_{n}$, squared scale $\sigma_{n}^{2} / \kappa_{n}$, and degrees of freedom $v_{n}$.

In the absence of strong prior information, a conventional choice of prior distribution is the Jeffreys' prior distribution:

$$
p\left(\mu, \sigma^{2}\right) \propto 1 / \sigma^{2}
$$

which is a degenerate special case of (6.25) with $\kappa_{0}=0, v_{0}=-1$, and $\sigma_{0}^{2}=0$. Substituting these values in Eqs. (6.26)-(6.28) yields $\mu_{n}=\bar{y}, \kappa_{n}=n, v_{n}=n- 1, \sigma_{n}^{2}=s^{2}$ and

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \sigma^{2} /\left((n-1) s^{2}\right) \mid \tilde{y} \sim \operatorname{Inv}-\chi^{2}(n-1) \\
& \left(\mu \mid \sigma^{2}, y\right) \sim N\left(\bar{y}, \sigma^{2} / n\right) \\
& (\mu \mid y) \sim t\left(\bar{y}, s^{2} / n, n-1\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

In particular, the $100(1-\alpha) \%$ posterior probability interval for $\mu$ given $y$ is $\bar{y} \pm t_{1-\alpha / 2} s / \sqrt{n}$, where $t_{1-\alpha / 2}$ is the $100(1-\alpha / 2)$ percentile of the Student $t$ distribution with center 0 , scale 1 , and degrees of freedom $n-1$. This interval is identical to the standard $100(1-\alpha) \%$ confidence interval for $\mu$. Thus, the Bayesian analysis with prior distribution (6.29) recovers the degrees of freedom correction and the $t$ reference distribution of the classical normal-sample frequentist analysis.

Example 6.17 Bayes' Inference for Unweighted and Weighted Multiple Linear Regression (Example 6.10 Continued). The Jeffreys' prior distribution for the normal linear regression model of Example 6.10 is

$$
p\left(\beta_{0}, \beta_{1}, \ldots, \beta_{p}, \sigma^{2}\right) \propto 1 / \sigma^{2}
$$

which places a uniform prior on the location parameters and $\ln \sigma$. The corresponding posterior distribution has the form

$$
\sigma^{2} /\left((n-p-1) s^{2}\right) \mid y \sim \operatorname{Inv}-\chi^{2}(n-p-1)
$$



$$
\begin{aligned}
& \left(\beta \mid \sigma^{2}, y\right) \sim N_{p+1}\left(\hat{\beta},\left(X^{\mathrm{T}} X\right)^{-1} \sigma^{2}\right) \\
& (\beta \mid y) \sim t_{p+1}\left(\hat{\beta},\left(X^{\mathrm{T}} X\right)^{-1} s^{2}, n-p-1\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

the multivariate $t$ distribution with center $\hat{\beta}$, scale $\left(X^{\mathrm{T}} X\right)^{-1} s^{2}$, and degrees of freedom $n-p-1$. Here, $s^{2}=n \hat{\sigma}^{2} /(n-p-1)$, the residual mean square adjusted for degrees of freedom. Equation (6.36) gives $100(1-\alpha) \%$ posterior probability intervals for the regression coefficients that match the confidence intervals from normal linear regression theory.

The extension to Bayes' inference for weighted multiple linear regression, where the residual variance of $y_{i}$ is $\sigma^{2} / w_{i}$ for known $w_{i}$, is straightforward; the only change is that $\left(y_{i}-\beta x_{i}\right) \sqrt{w_{i}} \sim_{\text {ind }} N\left(0, \sigma^{2}\right)$. Thus in (6.34)-(6.36), the weighted estimates (6.7) and (6.8) replace the unweighted estimates (6.5) and (6.6), and $X^{\mathrm{T}} W X$ replaces $X^{\mathrm{T}} X$, where $W$ is the diagonal matrix with entries $\left(w_{1}, \ldots, w_{n}\right)$.

The following two examples are important in practice.
Example 6.18 Bayes' Inference for a Multinomial Sample (Example 6.3 Continued). Suppose the data $y$ is a vector of counts $\left(n_{1}, \ldots, n_{C}\right)$ with a multinomial distribution as in Example 6.3. The conjugate prior distribution is the Dirichlet distribution, a multivariate generalization of the beta distribution:

$$
p\left(\pi_{1}, \ldots, \pi_{C}\right) \propto \prod_{c=1}^{C} \pi_{c}^{\alpha_{c}-1}, \quad \pi_{c}>0, \sum_{c=1}^{C} \pi_{c}=1
$$

Combining this prior distribution with the likelihood (6.3) yields the posterior distribution as Dirichlet with parameters $\left\{\tilde{n}_{c}+\alpha_{c}\right\}$ :

$$
p\left(\pi_{1}, \ldots, \pi_{C} \mid y\right) \propto \prod_{c=1}^{C} \pi_{c}^{n_{c}+\alpha_{c}-1}, \quad \pi_{c}>0, \sum_{c=1}^{C} \pi_{c}=1 .
$$

By properties of the Dirichlet distribution (Gelman et al. 2013, Appendix A), the posterior mean of $\pi_{c}$ is $\left(n_{c}+\alpha_{c}\right) /\left(n+\alpha_{+}\right)$, where $n=\sum_{c=1}^{C} n_{c}$, which equals the ML estimate when $\alpha_{c}=0$ for all $c$. Alternative relatively diffused prior distributions are given by $\alpha_{c}=1$ for all $c$, yielding the uniform distribution, or $\alpha_{c}=0.5$ for all $c$, which yields the Jeffreys' prior distribution for this problem.

Example 6.19 Bayes Inference for a Multivariate Normal Sample (Example 6.4 Continued). The noninformative Jeffreys' prior distribution for a multivariate normal sample with $\theta=(\mu, \Sigma)$ is

$$
p(\mu, \Sigma) \propto|\Sigma|^{-(K+1) / 2},
$$

which reduces to (6.29) when $K=1$. The corresponding posterior distribution can be written as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\Sigma /(n-1) \mid y) & \sim \text { Inv-Wishart }_{n-1}\left(S^{-1}\right) \\
(\mu \mid \Sigma, y) & \sim N_{K}(\bar{y}, \Sigma / n)
\end{aligned}
$$

where Inv-Wishart ${ }_{n-1}\left(S^{-1}\right)$ denotes the inverse Wishart distribution with $n-1$ degrees of freedom and scale matrix $S$ (see Gelman et al. 2013, Appendix A). Equation (6.40) implies that the marginal posterior distribution of $\mu$ is multivariate $t$ with center $\bar{y}$, scale matrix $S / n$, and degrees of freedom $n-1$.

### 6.1.5 Simulating Posterior Distributions

The application of Bayes' methods to more complex problems used to be constrained by the numerical difficulties involved when computing the posterior distribution of parameters, particularly when $\theta$ was high dimensional. For example if $\theta=\left(\theta_{1}, \theta_{2}\right)$, then the posterior distribution of $\theta_{1}$ is

$$
p\left(\theta_{1} \mid y\right)=\int p(\theta) L_{Y}(\theta \mid y) \mathrm{d} \theta_{2} / \int p(\theta) L_{Y}(\theta \mid y) \mathrm{d} \theta
$$

which involves high-dimensional integration when $\theta_{2}$ has many components. These problems have been reduced by simulation methods that take draws from the posterior distribution of $\theta$, rather than trying to compute the distribution analytically. These draws can be used to estimate characteristics of the posterior distribution of interest. For example, the mean and variance of the posterior distribution of scalar $\theta_{1}$ can be estimated as the sample mean, and variance of $D$ draws $\left(\theta_{1}^{(d)}, d=1, \ldots, D\right)$. If the posterior distribution is far from normal, $95 \%$ central probability intervals for $\theta_{1}$ can be estimated as the 2.5th to 97.5th percentiles of the empirical distribution of the draws $\left\{\theta_{1}^{(d)}\right\}$.

The transformation Property 6.1 for ML estimates has a direct analog for draws from the posterior distribution. We denote the analog as Property 6.1B, where B stands for "Bayes."

Property 6.1B Let $g(\theta)$ be a function of the parameter $\theta$, and let $\theta^{(d)}$ be the $d$ th draw from the posterior distribution of $\theta, d=1, \ldots, D$. Then, $g\left(\theta^{(d)}\right)$ is a draw from the posterior distribution of $g(\theta)$.

This property is useful in applications of Bayes simulation methods to incomplete-data problems, as discussed in Section 7.3 and Chapter 10.

Example 6.20 Bayes' Inference for Multiple Linear Regression (Example 6.17 Continued). Draws $\left\{\left(\beta^{(d)}, \sigma^{(d)}\right), d=1, \ldots, D\right\}$ from the posterior
distribution of $(\beta, \sigma)$ for the normal regression model data of Example 6.17 with prior distribution (6.33) are readily obtained from (6.34) and (6.35) as follows:

1. For $d=1, \ldots, D$, draw $\chi_{n-p-1}^{2}$ from a chi-squared distribution with $n-p-1$ degrees of freedom, and set

$$
\sigma^{(d) 2}=(n-p-1) s^{2} / \chi_{n-p-1}^{2} .
$$

2. Draw $p+1$ standard normal deviates, $z=\left(z_{0}, z_{1}, \ldots, z_{p}\right)^{\mathrm{T}}, z_{i} \sim N(0,1), i=0$, $1, \ldots, p$, and set

$$
\beta^{(d)}=\hat{\beta}+A^{\mathrm{T}} z \sigma^{(d)},
$$

where $A$ is an upper triangular ( $p \times p$ ) Cholesky factor of $\left(X^{\mathrm{T}} X\right)^{-1}$, such that $A^{\mathrm{T}} A=\left(X^{\mathrm{T}} X\right)^{-1}$. Draws are not needed for inference about the regression coefficients themselves, which have a $t$ distribution. However, they are often needed to simulate the posterior distribution of nonlinear functions of the parameters, using Property 6.1 B . For example, a draw from the posterior distribution of $\lambda=\beta_{1} / \beta_{2}$ is simply $\lambda^{(d)}=\beta_{1}^{(d)} / \beta_{2}^{(d)}$. The draws (6.41) and (6.42) play an important role for simulating posterior distributions for normal missing-data problems, discussed later.

Example 6.21 Bayes Inference for a Multinomial Sample (Example 6.18 Continued). Draws $\left\{\pi_{c}^{(d)}\right\}$ from the Dirichlet posterior distribution (6.38) of $\left\{\pi_{c}\right\}$ under the multinomial model of Example 6.18 can be obtained by generating independent chi-squared deviates $\left\{\chi_{2\left(n_{c}+\alpha_{c}\right)}^{2}\right\}$ for $c=1, \ldots, C$, and setting

$$
\pi_{c}^{(d)}=\chi_{2\left(n_{c}+\alpha_{c}\right)}^{2} / \sum_{j=1}^{C} \chi_{2\left(n_{j}+\alpha_{j}\right)}^{2}
$$

Often chi-squared random variables, and the associated $t$ distribution, are defined with integer degrees of freedom. This restriction is unnecessary because a more general form of (6.43) replaces the $\chi_{2\left(n_{c}+\alpha_{c}\right)}^{2}$ random variables by a standard (scale parameter $=1$ ) gamma distribution with parameters $n_{c}+\alpha_{c}$, with density proportional to $f\left(x \mid \alpha_{c}, n_{c}\right)=x^{n_{c}+\alpha_{c}-1} \exp (-x)$. See, for example, Gelman et al. (2013, Appendix A). For the special case of $C=2$, the multinomial sample is a binomial sample, and the Dirichlet prior and posterior distributions become beta prior and posterior distributions.

Example 6.22 Bayes' Inference for a Multivariate Normal Sample (Example 6.19 Continued). The posterior distribution of $\theta=(\mu, \Sigma)$ for a multivariate normal sample with prior distribution (6.39) is given by (6.40). A draw from this distribution is obtained by first drawing $C^{(d)}$ from Inv-Wishart ${ }_{n-1}\left(S^{-1}\right)$, setting
$\Sigma^{(d)}=(n-1) C^{(d)}$, and then drawing $\mu^{(d)}=\bar{y}+A^{(d) \mathrm{T}} z$, where $z=\left(z_{1}, \ldots, z_{K}\right)^{\mathrm{T}}$ is a vector of independent $N(0,1)$ draws, and $A^{(d)}$ is an upper triangular Cholesky factor such that $A^{(d) \mathrm{T}} A^{(d)}=\Sigma^{(d)} / n$.

The inverse-Wishart draw $C^{(d)}$ can be obtained by forming an upper triangular matrix $B$ with elements

$$
b_{j j} \sim \sqrt{\chi_{n-j}^{2}}, \quad b_{j k} \sim N(0,1), \quad j<k
$$

and drawing

$$
C^{(d)}=\left(B^{\mathrm{T}}\right)^{-1} A,
$$

where $A$ is the Cholesky factor of $S^{-1}$, that is $A^{\mathrm{T}} A=S^{-1}$. These results follow from the Bartlett decomposition of the Wishart distribution (e.g., Muirhead 1982).

### 6.2 Likelihood-Based Inference with Incomplete Data

In one high-level sense, there is no formal difference between ML or Bayes inference for incomplete data and ML or Bayes inference for complete data: The likelihood for the parameters based on the incomplete data is derived, ML estimates are found by solving the likelihood equation, and the posterior distribution is obtained by incorporating a prior distribution and performing the necessary integrations. Asymptotic standard errors obtained from the information matrix are somewhat more questionable with missing data, however, because the observed data do not generally constitute an iid sample, and simple results that imply the large sample normality of the likelihood function do not immediately apply. Other complications arise from dealing with the process that creates missing data. We will be somewhat imprecise in our treatment of these complications, to keep the notation simple. Rubin (1976a) gives a mathematically precise treatment, which also encompasses frequentist approaches that are not based on the likelihood; Mealli and Rubin (2015) provides an updated version for likelihood-based inference.

Let $Y=\left(y_{i j}\right), i=1, \ldots, n, j=1, \ldots, K$ denote the data matrix if there were no missing values, with $n$ units and $K$ variables, with $y_{i j} \in \Omega_{i j}$, its sample space. Let $M=\left(m_{i j}\right)$ denote the fully observed ( $n \times K$ ) matrix of binary missingness indicators with $m_{i j}=1$ if $y_{i j}$ is missing and $m_{i j}=0$ if $y_{i j}$ is observed. When $m_{i j}=1$, $y_{i j}=*$, indicating that $y_{i j}$ can take any value in $\Omega_{i j}$. We can model the density of the joint distribution of $Y$ and $M$ using the "selection model" factorization (Little and Rubin 2002)

$$
p(Y=y, M=m \mid \theta, \psi)=f_{Y}(y \mid \theta) f_{M \mid Y}(m \mid y, \psi),
$$

where $\theta$ is the parameter vector governing the data model, and $\psi$ is the parameter vector governing the model for the missingness mechanism. The observed value $m$ of $M$ effects a partition of $y=\left(y_{(0)}, y_{(1)}\right)$, where $y_{(0)}=\left[y_{i j}: m_{i j}=0\right]$ is the observed part of $y$ and $y_{(1)}=\left[y_{i j}: m_{i j}=1\right]$ is the missing part of $y .^{2}$

The full likelihood based on the observed values $\left(y_{(0)}, m\right)$ and the assumed model (6.46) is defined to be

$$
L_{\mathrm{full}}\left(\theta, \psi \mid y_{(0)}, m\right)=\int f_{Y}\left(y_{(0)}, y_{(1)} \mid \theta\right) f_{M \mid Y}\left(m \mid y_{(0)}, y_{(1)}, \psi\right) \mathrm{d} y_{(1)}
$$

considered as a function of the parameters $(\theta, \phi)$. The likelihood of $\theta$ ignoring the missingness mechanism is defined to be

$$
L_{\mathrm{ign}}\left(\theta \mid y_{(0)}\right)=\int f_{Y}\left(y_{(0)}, y_{(1)} \mid \theta\right) \mathrm{d} y_{(1)}
$$

which does not involve the model for $M$. The term "ignorable likelihood" is sometimes used for Eq. (6.48), hence the notation $L_{\text {ign }}$.

As discussed in detail in Chapter 15, modeling the joint distribution of $M$ and $Y$ is often challenging. Many approaches to missing data do not model $M$, and (implicitly or explicitly) base inference about $\theta$ on the ignorable likelihood, Eq. (6.48). It is thus important to consider under what conditions inferences about $\theta$ can be based on this simpler likelihood. The following definition addresses this question in general terms:

Definition 6.4 Let $\tilde{m}$ and $\tilde{y}_{(0)}$ denote particular realizations of $\left(m, y_{(0)}\right)$. The missingness mechanism is called ignorable if inferences for $\theta$ based on the ignorable likelihood equation (6.48) evaluated at $m=\tilde{m}, y_{(0)}=\tilde{y}_{(0)}$ are the same as inferences for $\theta$ based on the full likelihood equation (6.47) evaluated at $m=\tilde{m}, y_{(0)}=\tilde{y}_{(0)}$.

The conditions for ignoring the missingness mechanism depend on whether the inferences are direct likelihood, Bayes or frequentist. We consider each of these forms of inference in turn.

Direct likelihood inference, as discussed in Section 6.1.2, refers to inference based solely on likelihood ratios for pairs of values of the parameters, with the data fixed at their observed values. The missingness mechanism can be ignored for direct likelihood if the likelihood ratio based on the ignorable likelihood is the same as the ratio based on the full model. The following definition states this more precisely:

Definition 6.4A For the model defined by Eq. (6.46), the missingness mechanism is called ignorable for direct likelihood inference at ( $\tilde{m}, \tilde{y}_{(0)}$ ) if the
likelihood ratio for two values $\theta$ and $\theta^{*}$ is the same whether based on the full likelihood or the ignorable likelihood. That is

$$
\frac{L_{\mathrm{full}}\left(\theta, \psi \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}, \tilde{m}\right)}{L_{\mathrm{full}}\left(\theta^{*}, \psi \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}, \tilde{m}\right)}=\frac{L_{\mathrm{ign}}\left(\theta \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}\right)}{L_{\mathrm{ign}}\left(\theta^{*} \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}\right)} \quad \text { for all } \theta, \theta^{*}, \psi
$$

Theorem 6.1A The missingness mechanism is ignorable for direct likelihood inference $\left(\tilde{m}, \tilde{y}_{(0)}\right)$ if the following two conditions hold:
(a) Parameter distinctness: The parameters $\theta$ and $\psi$ are distinct, in the sense that the joint parameter space $(\theta, \psi)$, say $\Omega_{\theta, \psi}$, is the product of the parameter space $\Omega_{\theta}$ of $\theta$ and the parameter space $\Omega_{\psi}$ of $\psi$, that is $\Omega_{\theta, \psi}=\Omega_{\theta} \times \Omega_{\psi}$.
(b) Factorization of the full likelihood: The full likelihood, Eq. (6.47), with $\left(y_{0}, m\right)=\left(\tilde{y}_{0}, \tilde{m}\right)$ factors as

$$
L_{\text {full }}\left(\theta, \psi \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}, \tilde{m}\right)=L_{\text {ign }}\left(\theta \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}\right) \times L_{\text {rest }}\left(\psi \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}, \tilde{m}\right) \quad \text { for all } \theta, \psi \in \Omega_{\theta, \psi} .
$$

Theorem 6.1A follows directly after substituting Eq. (6.50) into the left side of Eq. (6.49). The distinctness condition ensures that each value of $\psi \in \Omega_{\psi}$ is compatible with different values of $\theta \in \Omega_{\theta}$, such as $\theta$ and $\theta^{*}$ in Eq. (6.49).

A sufficient condition for the likelihood to factor as in Eq. (6.50) is that the missing data are missing at random (MAR) at $\left(\tilde{m}, \tilde{y}_{(0)}\right)$, which is defined at an intuitive level in Section 1.3. Formally, the missing data are MAR at ( $\tilde{m}, \tilde{y}_{(0)}$ ) when the distribution function of $M$ evaluated at the observed values $\left(\tilde{y}_{(0)}, \tilde{m}\right)$ of ( $y_{(0)}, m$ ) does not depend on the missing values $y_{(1)}$, that is

$$
f_{M \mid Y}\left(\tilde{m} \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}, y_{(1)}, \psi\right)=f_{M \mid Y}\left(\tilde{m} \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}, y_{(1)}^{*}, \psi\right) \quad \text { for all } y_{(1)}, y_{(1)}^{*}, \psi
$$

Under Eq. (6.51),

$$
\begin{aligned}
& f\left(\tilde{y}_{(0)}, \tilde{m} \mid \theta, \psi\right)=f_{M \mid Y}\left(\tilde{m} \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}, \psi\right) \times \int f_{Y}\left(\tilde{y}_{(0)}, y_{(1)} \mid \theta\right) \mathrm{d} y_{(1)} \\
& =f_{M \mid Y}\left(\tilde{m} \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}, \psi\right) \times f_{Y}\left(\tilde{y}_{(0)} \mid \theta\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

yielding the factored likelihood (6.50). Thus, we have the following corollary:
Corollary 6.1A If the missing data are $M A R$ at $\left(\tilde{m}, \tilde{y}_{(0)}\right)$, and $\theta$ and $\psi$ are distinct, the missingness mechanism is ignorable for likelihood inference.

For Bayesian inference under the full model Eq. (6.46) for $Y$ and $M$, the full likelihood (6.49) is combined with a prior distribution $p(\theta, \psi)$ for $\theta$ and $\psi$

$$
p\left(\theta, \psi \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}, \tilde{m}\right) \propto p(\theta, \psi) \times L_{\text {full }}\left(\theta, \psi \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}, \tilde{m}\right)
$$

Bayesian inference ignoring the missingness mechanism combines the ignorable likelihood (6.48) with a prior distribution for $\theta$ alone, that is

$$
p\left(\theta \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}\right) \propto p(\theta) \times L_{\mathrm{ign}}\left(\theta \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}\right)
$$

We now provide parallel expressions to Definition 6.4A, Theorem 6.1A, and Corollary 6.1A for Bayesian inference:

Definition 6.4B The missingness mechanism is called ignorable for Bayesian inference at $\left(\tilde{m}, \tilde{y}_{(0)}\right)$ if the posterior distribution for $\theta$ based on Eq. (6.53) is the same as the posterior distribution for $\theta$ based on Eq. (6.52).

Theorem 6.1B The posterior distribution for $\theta$ based on Eq. (6.52) is the same as the posterior distribution for $\theta$ based on Eq. (6.53) if: (a) the parameters $\theta$ and $\psi$ are a priori independent, that is, the prior distribution has the form

$$
p(\theta, \psi)=p(\theta) p(\psi) ;
$$

and (b) the full likelihood evaluated at $\tilde{m}$ and $\tilde{y}_{0}$ factors as in Eq. (6.50).
Theorem 6.1B follows because under conditions (a) and (b),

$$
p\left(\theta, \psi \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}, \tilde{m}\right) \propto\left\{p(\theta) L_{\text {ign }}\left(\theta \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}\right)\right\} \times\left\{p(\psi) L_{\text {rest }}\left(\psi \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}, \tilde{m}\right)\right\}
$$

so the posterior distribution of $\theta$ based on Eq. (6.52) reduces to the posterior distribution given by Eq. (6.53). As for direct likelihood inference, MAR at ( $\tilde{m}, \tilde{y}_{(0)}$ ) is sufficient for condition (b), that the likelihood factors as in Eq. (6.50). Thus,

Corollary 6.1B If the missing data are MAR at ( $\tilde{m}, \tilde{y}_{(0)}$ ), and the parameters $\theta$ and $\psi$ are a priori independent, then the missingness mechanism is ignorable for Bayesian inference.

Note that the a priori independence condition, Eq. (6.54), is more stringent than the distinctness condition for direct likelihood inference because parameters with distinct parameter spaces might have dependent prior distributions. ${ }^{3}$

In order to ignore the missingness mechanism for asymptotic frequentist likelihood inference, the factorization Eq. (6.50) in general needs to be valid for values of the observed data in repeated sampling. That is, we require that

$$
\begin{aligned}
& L_{\text {full }}\left(\theta, \psi \mid y_{(0)}, m\right)=L_{\text {ign }}\left(\theta \mid y_{(0)}\right) \times L_{\text {rest }}\left(\psi \mid y_{(0)}, m\right) \\
& \quad \text { for all } y_{(0)}, m \text { and } \theta, \psi \in \Omega_{\theta, \psi^{\cdot}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For this form of inference, a sufficient condition for ignoring the missingness mechanism is a parameter distinctness, as in Definition 6.4, and that the missing data are missing always at random (MAAR), that is

$$
f_{M \mid Y}\left(m \mid y_{(0)}, y_{(1)}, \psi\right)=f_{M \mid Y}\left(m \mid y_{(0)}, y_{(1)}^{*}, \psi\right) \quad \text { for all } m, y_{(0)}, y_{(1)}, y_{(1)}^{*}, \psi
$$

For ease of presentation, in what follows, we discuss conditions for ignoring the missingness mechanism for direct likelihood and Bayes inference. However, we note that parallel conditions can be developed for frequentist likelihood inference, by requiring that the relevant equations apply for values of ( $m$, $\left.y_{(0)}\right)$, other than those observed that could arise in repeated sampling.

Example 6.23 Incomplete Exponential Sample. Suppose we have an incomplete univariate exponential sample with $y_{(0)}=\left(y_{1}, \ldots, y_{r}\right)^{\mathrm{T}}$ observed and $y_{(1)}=\left(y_{r+1}, \ldots, y_{n}\right)^{\mathrm{T}}$ missing. Thus, $m=\left(m_{1}, \ldots, m_{n}\right)^{\mathrm{T}}$, where $m_{i}=0, i=1, \ldots$, $r$ and $m_{i}=1, i=r+1, \ldots, n$. As in Example 6.2,

$$
f_{Y}(y \mid \theta)=\theta^{-n} \exp \left(-\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{y_{i}}{\theta}\right)
$$

The likelihood ignoring the missingness mechanism is

$$
L_{\mathrm{ign}}\left(\theta \mid y_{(0)}\right)=\theta^{-r} \exp \left(-\sum_{i=1}^{r} \frac{y_{i}}{\theta}\right)
$$

Suppose that each unit is observed with probability $\psi$ that does not depend on $Y$, so that Eq. (6.51) holds. Then,

$$
f_{M \mid Y}(m \mid y, \psi)=\frac{n!}{r!(n-r)!} \psi^{r}(1-\psi)^{n-r}
$$

and

$$
f\left(y_{(0)}, m \mid \theta, \psi\right)=\frac{n!}{r!(n-r)!} \psi^{r}(1-\psi)^{n-r} \theta^{-r} \exp \left(-\sum_{i=1}^{r} \frac{y_{i}}{\theta}\right)
$$

Because the missing data are MAR, if $\psi$ and $\theta$ are distinct, then likelihoodbased inferences about $\theta$ can be based on the ignorable likelihood, Eq. (6.57). In particular, the ML estimate of $\theta$ is simply $\sum_{i=1}^{r} y_{i} / r$, the mean of the observed values of $Y$.

Suppose, instead, that the incomplete data are created by censoring at some known censoring point $c$, so that only values less than $c$ are observed. Then,

$$
f_{M \mid Y}(m \mid y, \psi)=\prod_{i=1}^{n} f\left(m_{i} \mid y_{i}, \psi\right)
$$

where

$$
f\left(m_{i} \mid y_{i}, \psi\right)= \begin{cases}1, & \text { if } m_{i}=1 \text { and } y_{i} \geq c, \text { or } m_{i}=0 \text { and } y_{i}<c \\ 0, & \text { otherwise }\end{cases}
$$

Hence,

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\mathrm{full}}\left(\theta \mid y_{(0)}, m\right) & =\prod_{i=1}^{r} f_{Y}\left(y_{i} \mid \theta\right) \operatorname{Pr}\left(y_{i}<c \mid y_{i}, \theta\right) \times \prod_{i=r+1}^{n} \operatorname{Pr}\left(y_{i} \geq c \mid\right. \\
& =\theta^{-r} \exp \left(-\sum_{1}^{r} \frac{y_{i}}{\theta}\right) \times \exp \left(-\frac{(n-r) c}{\theta}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

because $\operatorname{Pr}\left(y_{i}<c \mid y_{i}, \theta\right)=1$ for respondents and $\operatorname{Pr}\left(y_{i} \geq c \mid \theta\right)=\exp (-c / \theta)$ for nonrespondents, using the properties of the exponential distribution. In this case, the missingness mechanism is not ignorable for likelihood inference, and the correct likelihood equation (6.58) differs from ignorable likelihood equation (6.57). Maximizing Eq. (6.58) with respect to $\theta$ gives the ML estimate $\hat{\theta}=\left(\sum_{i=1}^{r} y_{i}+(n-r) c\right) / r$, which can be compared with the (incorrect) ignorable ML estimate $\sum_{i=1}^{r} y_{i} / r$. The inflation of the sample mean in this expression reflects the censoring of the missing values.

Example 6.24 Bivariate Normal Sample with One Variable Subject to Missingness. Consider a bivariate normal sample, as in Example 6.9, but with the values $y_{i 2}$ of the second variable missing for $i=(r+1), \ldots, n$. We thus have a monotone pattern with two variables. The loglikelihood ignoring the missingness mechanism is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell_{\mathrm{ign}}\left(\mu, \Sigma \mid y_{(0)}\right)= & \log \left(L_{\mathrm{ign}}\left(\mu, \Sigma \mid y_{(0)}\right)\right)=-\frac{1}{2} r \ln |\Sigma|-\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{r}\left(y_{i}-\mu\right) \Sigma^{-1}\left(y_{i}-\mu\right)^{\mathrm{T}} \\
& -\frac{1}{2}(n-r) \ln \sigma_{11}-\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=r+1}^{n} \frac{\left(y_{i 1}-\mu_{1}\right)^{2}}{\sigma_{11}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This loglikelihood is appropriate for inferences provided the conditional distribution of $M$ (and in particular, the probability that $y_{i 2}$ is missing) does not depend on the values of $y_{i 2}$ (although it may depend on the values of $y_{i 1}$ ), and $\theta=(\mu, \Sigma)$ is distinct from $\psi$, the parameters of the missingness mechanism. Under these conditions, ML estimates of $\mu$ and $\Sigma$ can be found by maximizing (6.59). For Bayes inference, if these conditions hold and the prior distribution for $(\mu, \Sigma, \psi)$ has the form $p(\mu, \Sigma) p(\psi)$, then the joint posterior distribution of $\mu$ and $\Sigma$ is proportional to the product of $p(\mu, \Sigma)$ and $L_{\text {ign }}\left(\mu, \Sigma \mid y_{(0)}\right)$ from Eq. (6.59). A simple approach to these analyses based on factoring the likelihood is described in the next chapter.

With "random effects" models, there is a subtle interplay between the assumptions of MAR and distinctness (or a priori independence), depending on the definition of the hypothetical complete data. The following example illustrates aspects of this subtlety.

Example 6.25 One-Way ANOVA with Missing Values, When Missingness Depends on the Unobserved Group Means. Consider a one-way random effects ANOVA with $I$ groups, data $X=\left\{x_{i j}: i=1, \ldots, I ; j=1, \ldots, n_{i}\right\}$, and model

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \left(x_{i j} \mid \mu_{i}, \theta\right) \sim_{\text {ind }} N\left(\mu_{i}, \sigma^{2}\right), \\
& \left(\mu_{i} \mid \theta\right) \sim_{\text {ind }} N\left(\mu, \tau^{2}\right),
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\theta=\left(\mu, \sigma^{2}, \tau^{2}\right)$ are fixed unknown parameters; that is the unobserved mean for group $i, \mu_{i}$, is assumed to be sampled from the normal distribution (6.61), for $i=1, \ldots, I$. Suppose that some data values $x_{i j}$ are missing, and let $x_{(0)}$ and $x_{(1)}$ denote the observed and missing values of $X$, respectively. Suppose the missingness mechanism depends on the unobserved random variables $\left\{\mu_{i}\right\}$ :

$$
\operatorname{Pr}\left(m_{i j}=1 \mid x, \mu_{i}, \psi\right) \equiv \pi\left(\mu_{i}, \psi\right)=\exp \left(\psi_{0}+\psi_{1} \mu_{i}\right) /\left(1+\exp \left(\psi_{0}+\psi_{1} \mu_{i}\right)\right) .
$$

For likelihood inference, unknowns without a distribution are parameters, and unknowns with a distribution are missing data; see Section 6.3. Thus, the complete data must be defined to include the unobserved group means $\mu_{i}$, that is

$$
y=\left(x,\left\{\mu_{i}\right\}\right), y_{(0)}=x_{(0)}, \text { and } y_{(1)}=\left(x_{(1)},\left\{\mu_{i}\right\}\right),
$$

and the missingness mechanism (6.62) is nonignorable because the missing data are not MAR. Likelihood inference for the model defined by Eqs. (6.60)(6.62) must be based on the full likelihood, Eq. (6.47). Suppose that $r_{i}$ values of $X$ are observed in group $i, r=\sum_{i=1}^{I} r_{i}$, and let $\bar{x}_{(0) i}$ denote the mean of these observed values and $s_{(0) i}^{2}$ their sample variance with denominator $r_{i}$. The full likelihood is then

$$
\begin{aligned}
& L_{\text {full }}\left(\theta, \psi \mid y_{(0)}, m\right)=\sigma^{-r} \tau^{-I} \prod_{i=1}^{I} \int \pi\left(\mu_{i}, \psi\right)^{r_{i}}\left(1-\pi\left(\mu_{i}, \psi\right)\right)^{n_{i}-r_{i}} \\
& \quad \exp \left(-r_{i}\left(s_{(0) i}^{2}+\left(\bar{x}_{(0) i}-\mu_{i}\right)^{2}\right) /\left(2 \sigma^{2}\right)-\left(\mu_{i}-\mu\right)^{2} /\left(2 \tau^{2}\right)\right) \mathrm{d} \mu_{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

A common alternative model to Eqs. (6.60) and (6.61) is the fixed-effects ANOVA model

$$
x_{i j} \mid \mu_{i}, \sigma^{2} \sim_{\text {ind }} N\left(\mu_{i}, \sigma^{2}\right)
$$



where all components of $\theta^{*}=\left(\left\{\mu_{i}\right\}, \sigma^{2}\right)$ are regarded as the fixed unknown parameters. The data then do not include $\left\{\mu_{i}\right\}$ because they are fixed parameters, and the complete data are defined as

$$
y=\left(y_{(0)}, y_{(1)}\right), \quad \text { where } y_{(0)}=x_{(0)} \text { and } y_{(1)}=x_{(1)}
$$

The full likelihood with missingness mechanism (6.62) is then

$$
\begin{aligned}
& L_{\mathrm{full}}\left(\theta^{*}, \psi \mid y_{(0)}, m\right) \\
& \quad=\sigma^{-r} \prod_{i=1}^{I} \exp \left(-r_{i}\left(\bar{x}_{(0) i}-\mu_{i}\right)^{2} /\left(2 \sigma^{2}\right)\right) \pi\left(\mu_{i}, \psi\right)^{r_{i}}\left(1-\pi\left(\mu_{i}, \psi\right)\right)^{n_{i}-r_{i}}
\end{aligned}
$$

and the likelihood ignoring the missingness mechanism is then

$$
L_{\mathrm{ign}}\left(\theta^{*} \mid y_{(0)}\right)=\sigma^{-r} \prod_{i=1}^{I} \exp \left(-r_{i}\left(\bar{x}_{(0) i}-\mu_{i}\right)^{2} /\left(2 \sigma^{2}\right)\right)
$$

The missingness mechanism (6.62) is now MAR because (unlike in the random effects model) it does not depend on missing data. However, the distinctness condition in Definition 6.4 is violated because the models (6.62) and (6.63) both involve the parameters $\left\{\mu_{i}\right\}$. Hence, the missingness mechanism is nonignorable for likelihood inferences about $\theta^{*}$. Likelihood or Bayes inference ignoring the missingness mechanism, based on Eq. (6.65), is strictly speaking incorrect because a relevant part of the full likelihood equation (6.64) is being ignored. From the frequentist perspective, estimators based on Eq. (6.65) can be asymptotically unbiased despite the violation of the distinctness condition, but they are not generally efficient.

The sufficient conditions for ignoring the mechanism for likelihood inference discussed previously are for the full parameter vector $\theta$. Little et al. (2016a) propose the following definitions of partially MAR as well as ignorability for direct likelihood inferences for a subvector $\theta_{1}$ of the parameters $\theta$ in a model:

Definition 6.5 Write $\theta=\left(\theta_{1}, \theta_{2}\right)$, where $\theta_{1}$ and $\theta_{2}$ are subvectors of the components of the model for the data $X$ in Eq. (6.46). The data are partially MAR for direct likelihood inference about $\theta_{1}$, denoted $\mathrm{P}-\operatorname{MAR}\left(\theta_{1}\right)$, if the likelihood (6.47) can be factored as

$$
L_{\text {full }}\left(\theta_{1}, \theta_{2}, \psi \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}, \tilde{m}\right)=L_{1}\left(\theta_{1} \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}\right) \times L_{\text {rest }}\left(\theta_{2}, \psi \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}, \tilde{m}\right) \quad \text { for all } \theta_{1}, \theta_{2}, \psi
$$

where $L_{1}\left(\theta_{1} \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}\right)$ does not involve the model for the missingness mechanism, and $L_{\text {rest }}\left(\theta_{2}, \psi \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}, \tilde{m}\right)$ does not involve the parameters $\theta_{1}$.

Definition 6.6 The data are ignorable for direct likelihood inference about $\theta_{1}$, denoted $\operatorname{IGN}\left(\theta_{1}\right)$, if (i) the missingness mechanism is P-MAR( $\theta_{1}$ ) and (ii) $\theta_{1}$ and ( $\theta_{2}, \phi$ ) are distinct sets of parameters, in the sense given in Theorem 6.1A.

When the data are $\operatorname{IGN}\left(\theta_{1}\right)$, likelihood inference for $\theta_{1}$ based on $L_{1}\left(\theta_{1} \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}\right)$ is the same as inference under the full likelihood (6.66) because

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{L_{\text {full }}\left(\theta_{1}^{*}, \theta_{2}, \psi \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}, \tilde{m}\right)}{L_{\text {full }}\left(\theta_{1}^{* *}, \theta_{2}, \psi \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}, \tilde{m}\right)} & =\frac{L_{1}\left(\theta_{1}^{*} \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}\right)}{L_{1}\left(\theta_{1}^{* *} \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}\right)} \times \frac{L_{\text {rest }}\left(\theta_{2}, \psi \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}, \tilde{m}\right)}{L_{\text {rest }}\left(\theta_{2}, \psi \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}, \tilde{m}\right)} \\
& =\frac{L_{1}\left(\theta_{1}^{*} \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}\right)}{L_{1}\left(\theta_{1}^{* *} \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}\right)} \quad \text { for all } \theta_{1}^{*}, \theta_{1}^{* *}, \theta_{2}, \phi
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly, for Bayesian inference, if $\theta_{1}$ and $\left(\theta_{2}, \psi\right)$ are a priori independent, the posterior distribution of $\theta_{1}$ is proportional to $\pi_{1}\left(\theta_{1}\right) \times L_{1}\left(\theta_{1} \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}\right)$, where $\pi_{1}\left(\theta_{1}\right)$ is the prior distribution of $\theta_{1}$.

If the missingness mechanism is $\operatorname{P-MAR}\left(\theta_{1}\right)$, but $\theta_{1}$ and $\left(\theta_{2}, \phi\right)$ are not distinct parameters, partial likelihood inference based on $L_{1}\left(\theta_{1} \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}\right)$ is valid in certain asymptotic frequentist senses, and although not fully efficient, might still be entertained to avoid the additional assumptions involved in modeling the missingness mechanism. The partial likelihood $L_{1}\left(\theta_{1} \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}\right)$ combined with a prior distribution for $\theta_{1}$ yields a form of "pseudo-Bayesian" inference, which is not fully Bayes, but again avoids a model for the missingness mechanism. This approach to inference has been proposed and discussed in other contexts (for example Sinha and Ibrahim 2003; Ventura et al. 2009; Pauli et al. 2011).

Example 6.26 Regression Where Missingness Depends on the Covariates. Suppose the complete data are a random sample $\left(y_{i}, z_{i}\right), i=1, \ldots, n$ of values of variables $Y$ and $Z$, each of which may be vectors, and interest concerns the parameters $\theta_{1}$ of the regression of $Y$ on $Z$. Let $\left(y_{i(1)}, y_{i(0)}\right)$ and $\left(z_{i(1)}, z_{i(0)}\right)$ denote, respectively, the observed and missing components of $y_{i}$ and $z_{i}, i=1, \ldots, n$, and let $m_{i}=\left(m_{i}^{(Y)}, m_{i}^{(Z)}\right)$, where $m_{i}^{(Y)}$ and $m_{i}^{(Z)}$ are vectors of indicators for whether the components of $y_{i}$ and $z_{i}$ are missing, respectively. Suppose that for $i=1, \ldots, r, z_{i}$ is fully observed, and at least one component of $y_{i}$ is observed. For the remaining units, $i=r+1, \ldots, n, y_{i}$ is entirely missing, and the pattern of missing data for $z_{i}$ is arbitrary, but with at least one component of $z_{i}$ missing. We assume ( $y_{i}, z_{i}, m_{i}$ ) are iid across units, with

$$
\begin{aligned}
& f_{Y, Z, M}\left(y_{i}, z_{i}, m_{i} \mid \theta_{1}, \theta_{2}, \psi\right)=f_{Y \mid Z}\left(y_{i} \mid z_{i}, \theta_{1}\right) f_{Z}\left(z_{i} \mid \theta_{2}\right) \\
& \quad f_{M \mid Y, Z}\left(m_{i} \mid z_{i}, y_{i}, \psi\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

and also assume that the missingness mechanism depends on the covariates $Z$ but not on the outcomes $Y$, that is

$$
\begin{aligned}
& f_{M \mid Y, Z}\left(\tilde{m}_{i} \mid \tilde{z}_{i(1)}, z_{i(0)}, y_{i}, \psi\right) \\
& \quad=f_{M \mid Z}\left(\tilde{m}_{i} \mid \tilde{z}_{i(1)}, z_{i(0)}, y_{i}^{*}, \psi\right) \quad \text { for all } z_{i(0)}, y_{i}, y_{i}^{*}, i=1, \ldots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

This missingness mechanism is MNAR because missingness for $I=r+1, \ldots, n$ can depend on missing components of $Z$. The full likelihood factors as

$$
L\left(\theta_{1}, \theta_{2}, \psi\right)=L_{1}\left(\theta_{1} \mid \tilde{Y}_{(1)}, \tilde{Z}_{(1)}\right) \times L_{\mathrm{rest}}\left(\theta_{2}, \psi\right),
$$

where

$$
L_{1}\left(\theta_{1} \mid \tilde{Y}_{(0)}, \tilde{Z}_{(0)}\right)=\prod_{i=1}^{r} \int f_{Y \mid Z}\left(\tilde{y}_{i(0)}, y_{i(1)} \mid \tilde{z}_{i}, \theta_{1}\right) \mathrm{d} y_{i(1)}
$$

and

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text {rest }}\left(\theta_{2}, \psi\right)= & \prod_{i=1}^{r} f_{Z}\left(\tilde{z}_{i} \mid \theta_{2}\right) f_{M \mid Z}\left(\tilde{m}_{i} \mid \tilde{z}_{i}, \phi\right) \times \prod_{i=m+1}^{n} f_{Z}\left(\tilde{z}_{i(1)}, \tilde{z}_{i(0)} \mid \theta_{2}\right) \\
& \int f_{M \mid Z}\left(\tilde{m}_{i} \mid \tilde{z}_{i(0)}, z_{i(1)}, \psi\right) \mathrm{d} z_{i(1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, the data are P-MAR( $\theta_{1}$ ) and $\operatorname{IGN}\left(\theta_{1}\right)$ if $\theta_{1}$ and $\left(\theta_{2}, \phi\right)$ are distinct parameters. Thus, valid inferences for $\theta_{1}$ can be based on the data in the first $m$ cases without modeling the missingness mechanism, though there may be information about $\left(\theta_{2}, \phi\right)$ lost in the discarded data. For the special case where the first $m$ units have no missing values of $Y$, this is complete-case analysis, which is known to be valid in a frequentist sense, as discussed in Example 3.3.

### 6.3 A Generally Flawed Alternative to Maximum Likelihood: Maximizing over the Parameters and the Missing Data

### 6.3.1 The Method

A different approach to handling incomplete data, occasionally encountered in the literature, is to treat the missing data as parameters and to maximize the complete data likelihood over both the missing data and parameters. That is, let

$$
L_{\text {mispar }}\left(\theta, y_{(1)} \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}\right)=f_{Y}\left(\tilde{y}_{(0)}, y_{(1)} \mid \theta\right)
$$

be regarded as a function of $\left(\theta, y_{(1)}\right)$ for fixed $\tilde{y}_{(0)}$, and estimate $\theta$ by maximizing $L_{\text {mispar }}\left(\theta, y_{(1)} \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}\right)$ over both $\theta$ and $y_{(1)}$. When the missing data are not MAR, or $\theta$ is not distinct from $\psi, \theta$ would be estimated in this approach by maximizing

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text {mispar }}\left(\theta, \psi, y_{(1)} \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}, \tilde{m}\right) & =L_{\text {full }}\left(\theta, \psi \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}, y_{(1)}, \tilde{m}\right) \\
& =f_{Y}\left(\tilde{y}_{(0)}, y_{(1)} \mid \theta\right) f_{M \mid Y}\left(\tilde{m} \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}, y_{(1)}, \psi\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

over $\left(\theta, \psi, y_{(1)}\right)$. Although this approach can be useful in particular problems, such as described in Chapter 2, it is not a generally valid approach to the analysis of incomplete data. The optimal frequentist properties of ML estimation require that the number of parameters increases at a slow enough rate relative to the sample size. This condition implies that maximizing $L_{\text {mispar }}$ over $(\theta, \psi$, $\left.y_{(1)}\right)$ is only generally valid under asymptotics where the proportion of missing data goes to zero as the sample size increases, a form of asymptotics that is not relevant because it does not reflect the loss of information from missing data. In other words, maximizing $L_{\text {mispar }}$ over $\left(\theta, \psi, y_{(1)}\right)$ is an example of situation S2 in Section 6.1.3, where the usual asymptotics of ML do not apply.

### 6.3.2 Background

The classic example of the approach in Section 6.3.1 is the treatment of missing plots in the ANOVA where missing outcomes $y_{(1)}$ are treated as parameters and estimated along with the model parameters to allow computationally efficient methods to be used for analysis (see Chapter 2). DeGroot and Goel (1980) propose this approach as one possibility for the analysis of a mixed-up bivariate normal sample, where the missing data are the indices that allow the values of the two variables to be paired, and a priori all pairings are assumed equally likely.
