Press and Scott (1976) present a Bayesian analysis of an incomplete multivariate normal sample, which is equivalent to maximizing $L_{\text {mispar }}$ in Eq. (6.68) over ( $\theta, y_{(1)}$ ). Box et al. (1970) and Bard (1974) apply the same approach in a more general setting where the multivariate normal mean vector has a nonlinear regression on covariates. Lee and Nelder (1996) advocate this approach for the analysis of generalized linear mixed models, as discussed in Example 6.30 below.

Formally, $L_{\text {full }}\left(\theta, \psi \mid y_{(0)}, y_{(1)}, m\right)$ defined in Eq. (6.47), or $L_{\text {ign }}\left(\theta \mid y_{(0)}\right)$ defined in Eq. (6.48) if the missingness mechanism is ignorable, define the correct likelihood for inferences about $\theta$ based on the observed data; the functions $L_{\text {mispar }}$ in Eqs. (6.68) or (6.69) are not likelihoods because their arguments include random variables $y_{(1)}$, which have a distribution under the specified model and hence should not be treated as fixed parameters. Thus, maximization of $L_{\text {mispar }}$ with respect to $\theta$ and $Y_{\text {mis }}$ is not an ML procedure, and as noted in the previous section, it does not generally enjoy the optimal properties of ML. The deficiencies of treating $y_{(1)}$ as a parameter are illustrated in the following examples.

### 6.3.3 Examples

Example 6.27 Univariate Normal Sample with Missing Data. Suppose that $y=\left(y_{(0)}, y_{(1)}\right)$ consists of $n$ realizations from a normal distribution with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^{2}$, where $y_{(0)}$ consists of $r$ observed values, and $y_{(1)}$ consists of $(n-r)$ missing values, which are assumed MAR; $\theta$ is $\left(\mu, \sigma^{2}\right)$, which we assume is distinct from the parameters of the missingness mechanism. The ignorable likelihood $L_{\text {ign }}\left(\theta \mid y_{(0)}\right)$ is the likelihood for a sample of size $r$ from a normal distribution, and maximizing it over $\theta$ yields ML estimates

$$
\hat{\mu}=\sum_{i=1}^{r} \frac{y_{i}}{r} \quad \text { and } \quad \hat{\sigma}^{2}=\sum_{i=1}^{r} \frac{\left(y_{i}-\hat{\mu}\right)^{2}}{r} .
$$

Equation (6.68) for this model is

$$
\begin{aligned}
& L_{\text {mispar }}\left(\mu, \sigma^{2}, y_{r+1}, \ldots, y_{n} \mid y_{1}, \ldots, y_{r}\right) \\
& \quad=\left(2 \pi \sigma^{2}\right)^{-n / 2} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{r} \frac{\left(y_{i}-\mu\right)^{2}}{\sigma^{2}}\right\} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=r+1}^{n} \frac{\left(y_{i}-\mu\right)^{2}}{\sigma^{2}}\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

and maximizing (6.69) over $\mu, \sigma^{2}, y_{r+1}, \ldots, y_{n}$ yields estimates $\hat{\mu}^{*}, \hat{\sigma}^{* 2}, \hat{y}_{r+1}$, $\ldots, \hat{y}_{n}$ where

$$
\hat{y}_{i}=\hat{\mu}, i=r+1, \ldots, n, \quad \hat{\mu}^{*}=\hat{\mu}, \quad \hat{\sigma}^{* 2}=r \hat{\sigma}^{2} / n
$$

where $\hat{\mu}, \hat{\sigma}^{2}$ are the ML estimates (6.70). Thus, maximizing $L_{\text {mispar }}$ yields the ML estimate of the mean, but the estimate of the variance is the ML estimate multiplied by the fraction of data observed, $r / n$. When the fraction of missing data is substantial (e.g., $(n-r) / n=0.5$ ), the estimated variance is badly biased, and this bias does not disappear as $n \rightarrow \infty$ unless $r / n \rightarrow 1$; more relevant asymptotics would fix $r / n$ as the sample size increases.

Example 6.28 Missing Plot Analysis of Variance. Suppose we add to the previous example a set of covariates $X$ that is observed for all $n$ observations. We assume that the outcome $y_{i}$ for observation $i$ with covariate values $x_{i}$ is normal with mean $\beta_{0}+x_{i} \beta$ and variance $\sigma^{2}$, and write $\theta=\left(\beta_{0}, \beta, \sigma^{2}\right)$. The estimates of $\beta_{0}, \beta$, and $\sigma^{2}$ that maximize the likelihood $L_{\text {ign }}\left(\theta \mid y_{(0)}\right)$ are obtained by applying least squares regression to the data from the $r$ observed units. The estimates of $\beta_{0}$ and $\beta$ obtained by maximizing $L_{\text {mispar }}$ are the same as the ML estimates. As in Example 6.26, however, the estimate of variance is the ML estimate multiplied by the proportion of values observed.

Example 6.29 An Exponential Sample with Censored Values. In Examples 6.27 and 6.28, estimation based on maximizing $L_{\text {mispar }}$ yields reasonable estimates of the location parameters, even though estimates of the scale parameter generally need adjustment. In other examples, however, estimates of location also can be badly biased. For example, consider, as in Example 6.23, a censored sample from an exponential distribution with mean $\theta$, where $y_{(0)}$ represents the $r$ observed values, which lie below a known censoring point $c$, and $y_{(1)}$ represents the $n-r$ values beyond $c$, which are censored. As discussed in Example 6.23, the ML estimate of $\theta$ is $\hat{\theta}=\bar{y}+(n-r) c / r$. Maximizing $L_{\text {mispar }}$ in Eq. (6.68) over $\theta$ and $y_{(1)}$ leads to estimating censored values of $\hat{y}_{i}=c, i=r+1, \ldots, n$, and estimating $\theta$ by $(r / n) \hat{\theta}$. Thus, in this example, the estimate of the mean is inconsistent unless the proportion of missing values tends to zero as the sample size increases.

Example 6.30 ML Estimation for Generalized Linear Mixed Models. Breslow and Clayton (1993) consider an extension of the Generalized Linear Model of Example 6.11 to include random effects. Suppose that conditional on covariates $x_{i}$ and an unobserved random effect $u_{i}$ for unit $i$, the outcome $y_{i}$ has the distribution of Eq. (6.9), that is

$$
f\left(y_{i} \mid x_{i}, u_{i}, \beta, \phi\right)=\exp \left[\left(y_{i} \delta\left(x_{i}, u_{i}, \beta\right)-b\left(\delta\left(x_{i}, u_{i}, \beta\right)\right)\right) / \phi+c\left(y_{i}, \phi\right)\right] .
$$

The mean of $y_{i}$ given $x_{i}$ and $u_{i}, \mu_{i}=E\left(y_{i} \mid x_{i}, u_{i}, \beta, \phi\right)$, is related to $u_{i}$ and the covariates $x_{i}$ by the expression

$$
\mu_{i}=g^{-1}\left(\beta_{0}+\sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_{j} x_{i j}+u_{i}\right),
$$

where $g(\cdot)$ is the link function. Also, the $u_{i}$ are assumed independent with density $f\left(u_{i} \mid x_{i}, \alpha\right)$ indexed by unknown parameters $\alpha$. The likelihood given data $y_{(0)}=\left(y_{1}, y_{2}, \ldots, y_{n}\right)$ is then:

$$
L\left(\beta, \phi, \alpha \mid y_{(0)}\right)=\prod_{i=1}^{n} \int f\left(y_{i} \mid x_{i}, u_{i}, \beta, \phi\right) f\left(u_{i} \mid x_{i}, \alpha\right) \mathrm{d} u_{i},
$$

which is complicated by the integration over the unobserved random effects $u_{i}$, which must be viewed as missing values for likelihood inference. Lee and Nelder (1996) avoid the integration by maximizing

$$
h(\beta, \phi, \alpha, u \mid Y)=\sum_{i=1}^{n} \log \left(f\left(y_{i} \mid x_{i}, u_{i}, \beta, \phi\right)\right)+\log \left(f\left(u_{i} \mid x_{i}, \alpha\right)\right),
$$

with respect to $(\beta, \phi, \alpha)$ and $u=\left(u_{1}, \ldots, u_{n}\right)^{\mathrm{T}}$. This is the logarithm of $L_{\text {mispar }}$ in Eq. (6.68), with $\theta=(\beta, \phi, \alpha), y_{(0)}=\left(y_{1}, \ldots, y_{n}\right)$, and $y_{(1)}=\left(u_{1}, \ldots, u_{n}\right)$. When
both distributions are normal, (6.76) is the joint loglikelihood maximized by Henderson (1975). Unlike maximization of (6.75), maximization of (6.76) does not generally give consistent estimates of the parameters (Breslow and Lin 1995), especially for problems involving nonconjugate distributions for $u_{i}$. Algorithms for maximizing the loglikelihood (6.75) are discussed in Breslow and Clayton (1993), McCulloch (1997), and Aitkin (1999).

To address this issue, Lee and Nelder (2001) and Lee et al. (2006) propose maximizing a modification of (6.76), namely a function they call the "adjusted profile $h$-likelihood"

$$
\operatorname{APHL}(\beta, \phi, \alpha)=\left.[h(\beta, \phi, \alpha, u \mid Y)-0.5 \log \operatorname{det}\{D(h, u) / 2 \pi\}]\right|_{u=\tilde{u}},
$$

where $D(h, u)=-\partial^{2} h / \partial u^{2}$ and $\tilde{u}$ solves $\partial h / \partial u=0$. This approach can be viewed as a numerical (Laplace) approximation to maximizing (6.75), which is the correct ML approach. For more details, see Lee and Nelder (2009) and the discussion, particularly Meng (2009).

### 6.4 Likelihood Theory for Coarsened Data

Missing values are a form of "data coarsening." Heitjan and Rubin (1990) and Heitjan (1994) develop a more general theory for coarsened data, which includes heaped, censored, and grouped data as well as missing data. Denoted by $Y=\left\{y_{i j}\right\}$, the complete data matrix in the absence of coarsening, and let $f_{Y}(y \mid \theta)$ denote the density of $Y$ under a complete data model with unknown parameter $\theta$. The observed data, say $y_{i j(0)}$, for each value $y_{i j}$ are that $y_{i j}$ lies in a subset of its sample space $\Psi_{i j}$, which is a determined by a function of $y_{i j}$ and a coarsening variable $c_{i j}$, that is $y_{i j(0)}=y_{i j(0)}\left(y_{i j}, c_{i j}\right)$, subject to the condition that the coarsened subset contains the unobserved true value, that is $y_{i j} \in y_{i j(0)}\left(y_{i j}\right.$, $c_{i j}$ ). For the special case of missing data discussed so far, $C=\left\{c_{i j}\right\}$ is simply the matrix of binary missingness indicators, and

$$
y_{i j(0)}=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}
\left\{y_{i j}\right\}, \text { the set consisting of the single true value, } & \text { if } c_{i j}=0, \\
\Psi_{i j}, \text { the sample space of } y_{i j}, & \text { if } c_{i j}=1 .
\end{array}\right.
$$

Example 6.31 Censoring with Stochastic Censoring Time. Suppose $Y$ is time to an event, and some values of $Y$ are observed, and others are known to be censored. For observation $i$, let $y_{i}$ be the value of $Y$, and $c_{i}$ the value of the stochastic censoring time $C$. The complete data are $\left(y_{i}, c_{i}\right), i=1, \ldots, n$. The coarsened data for subject $i$ are

$$
y_{(0) i}=y_{(0) i}\left(y_{i}, c_{i}\right)= \begin{cases}\left\{y_{i}\right\}, & \text { if } y_{i} \leq c_{i} \\ \left(c_{i}, \infty\right), & \text { if } y_{i}>c_{i}\end{cases}
$$

That is if the event occurs before the censoring point, then $y_{(0) i}$ is the point set consisting of the actual event time $y_{i}$, and if the event occurs after the censoring time, then $y_{(0) i}$ is the set of times beyond $c_{i}$. Note that when $y_{i}$ is observed, the corresponding censoring time $c_{i}$ is not observed, unlike the missing-data case where the missingness indicator is always observed.

Uncertainty in the degree of coarsening is modeled by assigning $C$ a probability distribution with conditional density given $Y=y$ equal to $f_{C \mid Y}(c \mid y, \phi)$. Write $y=\left(y_{(0)}, y_{(1)}\right)$ and $c=\left(c_{(0)}, c_{(1)}\right)$, where $y_{(0)}$ and $y_{(1)}$ are the observed and missing components of $Y$, and $c_{(0)}$ and $c_{(1)}$ are the observed and missing components of $C$. The full coarsened data likelihood is then

$$
L_{\text {full }}\left(\theta, \phi \mid y_{(0)}, c_{(0)}\right)=\iint f_{C \mid Y}\left(c_{(0)}, c_{(1)} \mid y_{(0)}, y_{(1)}, \phi\right) f_{Y}\left(y_{(0)}, y_{(1)} \mid \theta\right) \mathrm{d} y_{(1)} \mathrm{d} c_{(1)}
$$

and the likelihood ignoring the coarsening mechanism is

$$
L_{\mathrm{ign}}\left(\theta \mid y_{(0)}\right)=\int f_{Y}\left(y_{(0)}, y_{(1)} \mid \theta\right) \mathrm{d} y_{(1)}
$$

The following definitions and lemma generalize the ideas of MAR and ignorable missingness mechanisms to coarsened data:

Definition 6.7 The data are coarsened at random (CAR) at the observed values $y_{(0)}=\tilde{y}_{(0)}, c_{(0)}=\tilde{c}_{(0)}$ if

$$
\begin{aligned}
& f_{C \mid Y}\left(\tilde{c}_{(0)}, c_{(1)} \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}, y_{(1)}, \phi\right)=f_{C \mid Y}\left(\tilde{c}_{(0)}, c_{(1)}^{*} \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}, y_{(1)}^{*}, \phi\right) \\
& \quad \text { for all } c_{(1)}, c_{(1)}^{*}, y_{(1)}, y_{(1)}^{*}, \phi
\end{aligned}
$$

Definition 6.8 The coarsening mechanism is ignorable if inference for $\theta$ based on $L_{\text {ign }}$ is equivalent to inference based on the full likelihood $L_{\text {full }}$.

Conditions for ignoring the coarsening mechanism parallel the conditions for ignoring the missingness mechanism, described previously in Theorems 6.1A and 6.1B and Corollaries 6.1A and 6.1B. In particular, sufficient conditions for ignoring the coarsening mechanism for likelihood inference at $\tilde{y}_{(0)}$ and $\tilde{c}_{(0)}$ are that (i) the data are CAR and (ii) the parameters $\theta$ and $\phi$ are distinct. Sufficient conditions for ignoring the coarsening mechanism for Bayesian inference are that (i) the data are CAR and (ii) the parameters $\theta$ and $\phi$ have independent prior distributions.

Example 6.32 Censoring Mechanisms (Example 6.30 Continued). For the case of censored data and $\left(y_{i}, c_{i}\right)$ independent of $\left(y_{j}, c_{j}\right)$ when $i \neq j$, the full likelihood equation (6.78) is

$$
\begin{gathered}
L_{\mathrm{full}}\left(\theta, \phi \mid y_{(0)}, c_{(0)}\right)=\prod_{i: c_{i} \geq y_{i}} \int_{c_{i}>y_{i}} f_{Y}\left(y_{i} \mid x_{i}, \theta\right) f_{C \mid Y}\left(c_{i} \mid y_{i}, x_{i}, \phi\right) \mathrm{d} c_{i} \prod_{i: c_{i}<y_{i}} \\
\left(\int_{y_{i}>c_{i}} f_{C \mid Y}\left(c_{i} \mid y_{i}, x_{i}, \phi\right) f_{Y}\left(y_{i} \mid x_{i}, \theta\right) \mathrm{d} y_{i}\right)
\end{gathered}
$$

where $x_{i}$ denotes a set of fully observed covariates for unit $i$, with $f_{Y}\left(y_{i} \mid x_{i}, \theta\right)$ the density of $y_{i}$ given $x_{i}$ and $f_{C \mid Y}\left(c_{i} \mid y_{i}, x_{i}, \phi\right)$ the density of $c_{i}$ given $\left(x_{i}, y_{i}\right)$. The likelihood equation (6.79) ignoring the coarsening mechanism is

$$
L_{\mathrm{ign}}\left(\theta \mid y_{(0)}\right)=\prod_{i: c_{i} \geq y_{i}} f_{Y}\left(y_{i} \mid x_{i}, \theta\right) \prod_{i: c_{i}<y_{i}}\left(\int_{y_{i}>c_{i}} f_{Y}\left(y_{i} \mid x_{i}, \theta\right) \mathrm{d} y_{i}\right)
$$

The data are CAR at the observed data $\left\{\left(\tilde{c}_{i}, \tilde{y}_{i}, \tilde{x}_{i}\right), i=1, \ldots, n\right\}$ if

$$
f_{C}\left(\tilde{c}_{i} \mid \tilde{y}_{i}, \tilde{x}_{i}, \phi\right)=f_{C}\left(\tilde{c}_{i} \mid \tilde{x}_{i}, \phi\right) \quad \text { for all } \phi, i=1, \ldots, n
$$

because otherwise, in general, the integrals in Eq. (6.80) cannot get passed over the first factors to yield Eq. (6.81). Hence, the censoring mechanism cannot depend on the values of the outcome $Y$, although it can depend on the values of the covariates. If the distinctness condition is also satisfied, then the censoring mechanism is ignorable for likelihood inference. Note that the censoring mechanism is CAR but not MAR under these conditions. For more discussion, see Heitjan (1994) and Jacobsen and Keiding (1995).

## Problems

6.1 Write the likelihood function for an iid sample from the (a) beta distribution; (b) Poisson distribution; and (c) Cauchy distribution with location $\theta$ and scale 1.
6.2 Find the score function for the distributions in Problem 6.1. Find the ML estimates for those distributions that have closed-form estimates.
6.3 For a univariate normal sample, find the ML estimate of the coefficient of variation, $\sigma / \mu$.
6.4 (a) Compare ML and least squares estimates for the model of Example 6.10.
(b) Show that if the data are iid with the Laplace (double exponential) distribution,

$$
f\left(y_{i} \mid \theta\right)=0.5 \exp \left(-\left|y_{i}-\mu\left(x_{i}\right)\right|\right),
$$

where $\mu\left(x_{i}\right)=\beta_{0}+\beta_{1} x_{i 1}+\cdots+\beta_{p} x_{i p}$, then ML estimates of $\beta_{0}, \ldots, \beta_{p}$ are obtained by minimizing the sum of absolute deviations of the $y$ values from their expected values.
6.5 Suppose the data are a random sample of size $n$ from the uniform distribution between 0 and $\theta, \theta>0$. Show that the ML estimate of $\theta$ is the largest data value. (Hint: Differentiation of the score function does not work for this problem!) Find the posterior mean of $\theta$, assuming a uniform prior distribution for $\theta$. Which of these estimators do you prefer for this problem and why?
6.6 Show that for the GLIM model of Eq. (6.9), $E\left(y_{i} \mid x_{i}, \beta\right)=b^{\prime}\left(\delta\left(x_{i}, \beta\right)\right)$, where prime denotes differentiation with respect to the function's argument. Conclude that the canonical link Eq. (6.12) is obtained by setting $g^{-1}(\cdot)=b^{\prime}(\cdot)$. (Hint: Consider the density of $y_{i}$ in Eq. (6.9) as a function of $\delta_{i}=\delta\left(x_{i}, \beta\right)$ and differentiate the expression $\int f\left(y_{i} \mid \delta_{i}, \phi\right) \mathrm{d} y_{i}=1$ with respect to $\delta_{i}$; you may assume that the derivative can be passed through the integral sign.)
6.7 Show, by similar arguments to those in Problem 6.6, that for the model of Eq. (6.9), $\operatorname{Var}\left(y_{i} \mid \delta_{i}, \phi\right)=\phi b^{\prime \prime}\left(\delta_{i}\right)$, where $\delta_{i}=\delta\left(x_{i}, \beta\right)$, and double prime denotes differentiation twice with respect to the function's argument.
6.8 Summarize the theoretical and practical differences between the frequentist and Bayesian interpretation of Approximation 6.1. Which is closer to the direct-likelihood interpretation?
6.9 For the distributions of Problem 6.1, calculate the observed information and the expected information.
6.10 Show that, for random samples from "regular" distributions (differentials can be passed through the integral), the expected squared-score function equals the expected information.
6.11 In Example 6.15, show that for large $n, \mathrm{LR}=t^{2}$.



6.12 Derive the posterior distributions in Eqs. (6.26)-(6.28) for Example 6.16.
6.13 Derive the posterior distributions in Eqs. (6.34)-(6.36) for Example 6.17.
6.14 Derive the modifications of the posterior distributions in Eqs. (6.34)(6.36) for weighted linear regression, discussed at the end of Example 6.17. Show that for the special case of weighted linear regression with no intercept $\left(\beta_{0}=0\right)$, a single covariate $X$, and weight for observation $i w_{i}=x_{i}$, the ratio estimator $\bar{y} / \bar{x}$ is (a) the ML estimate of $\beta_{1}$ and (b) the posterior mean of $\beta_{1}$ when the prior distribution for $\left(\beta_{1}, \sigma^{2}\right)$ is $p\left(\beta_{1}, \log \sigma^{2}\right)=$ const.
6.15 Suppose the following data are a random sample of $n=7$ from the Cauchy distribution with median $\theta: Y=(-4.2,-3.2,-2.0,0.5,1.5,1.5$, 3.5). Compute and compare $90 \%$ intervals for $\theta$ using (a) the asymptotic distribution based on the observed information, (b) the asymptotic distribution based on the expected information, and (c) the posterior distribution assuming a uniform prior distribution for $\theta$.
6.16 Find large-sample sampling variance estimates for the two ML estimates in Example 6.23.
6.17 For a bivariate normal sample $\left(y_{i 1}, y_{i 2}\right), i=1, \ldots, n$ on $\left(Y_{1}, Y_{2}\right)$ with parameters $\theta=\left(\mu_{1}, \mu_{2}, \sigma_{11}, \sigma_{12}, \sigma_{22}\right)$, missing values of $Y_{2}$, and $m_{i 2}$ the missingness indicator for $y_{i 2}$, state for the following missingness mechanisms (i) whether the data are MAR and (ii) whether the missingness mechanism is ignorable for likelihood-based inference.
(a) $\operatorname{Pr}\left(m_{i 2}=1 \mid y_{i 1}, y_{i 2}, \theta, \psi\right)=\exp \left(\psi_{0}+\psi_{1} y_{i 1}\right) /\left\{1+\exp \left(\psi_{0}+\psi_{1} y_{i 1}\right)\right\}$, $\psi=\left(\psi_{0}, \psi_{1}\right)$ distinct from $\theta$.
(b) $\operatorname{Pr}\left(m_{i 2}=1 \mid y_{i 1}, y_{i 2}, \theta, \psi\right)=\exp \left(\psi_{0}+\psi_{1} y_{i 2}\right) /\left\{1+\exp \left(\psi_{0}+\psi_{1} y_{i 2}\right)\right\}$, $\psi=\left(\psi_{0}, \psi_{1}\right)$ distinct from $\theta$.
(c) $\operatorname{Pr}\left(m_{i 2}=1 \mid y_{i 1}, y_{i 2}, \theta, \psi\right)=0.5 \exp \left(\mu_{1}+\psi y_{i 1}\right) /\left\{1+\exp \left(\mu_{1}+\psi y_{i 1}\right)\right\}$, scalar $\psi$ distinct from $\theta$.
6.18 Suppose that, given sets of covariates $X_{1}$ and $X_{2}$ (possibly overlapping, that is, not disjoint), $y_{i 1}$ and $y_{i 2}$ are bivariate normal with means $x_{i 1} \beta_{1}$ and $x_{i 2} \beta_{2}$, variances $\sigma_{1}^{2}$ and $\sigma_{2}^{2}=1$, and correlation $\rho$. The data consist of a random sample of units $i$ with $x_{i 1}$ and $x_{i 2}$ always observed, $y_{i 2}$ always missing and $y_{i 1}$ missing if and only if $y_{i 2}>0$. Let $m_{i 1}$ be the missingness indicator for $y_{i 1}$. Show that

$$
\operatorname{Pr}\left(m_{i 1}=1 \mid y_{i 1}, x_{i 1}, x_{i 2}, \theta, \psi\right)=1-\Phi\left(\frac{-x_{i 2} \beta_{2}-\left(\rho / \sigma_{1}\right)\left(y_{i 1}-x_{i 1} \beta_{1}\right)}{\sqrt{1-\rho^{2}}}\right),
$$

where $\Phi$ is the standard normal cumulative distribution function. Hence, give conditions on the parameters under which the data are MAR and under which the missingness mechanism is ignorable for likelihoodbased inference. (This model is considered in detail in Example 15.5.)
6.19 The definition of MAR can depend on how the complete data are defined. Suppose that $X=\left(x_{1}, \ldots, x_{n}\right), Z=\left(z_{1}, \ldots, z_{n}\right)\left(x_{i}, z_{i}\right)$ are a random sample from a bivariate normal distribution with means ( $\mu_{x}, 0$ ), variances ( $\sigma_{x}^{2}, 1$ ), and correlation 0 (so $x_{i}$ and $z_{i}$ are independent). Suppose that some values $x_{i}$ are missing, the missingness indicator for $x_{i}$ is $m_{i}, Z$ are completely unobserved latent variables, and the missingness mechanism is given by

$$
\operatorname{Pr}\left(m_{i}=1 \mid x_{i}, z_{i}\right)=\exp \left(z_{i}\right) /\left(1+\exp \left(z_{i}\right)\right) .
$$

Show that if the complete data are defined as $X$, then the missing data are missing completely at random (MCAR), but if the complete data are defined as $(X, Z)$, then the missing data are not MAR. Which is the more sensible definition?
6.20 For Example 6.27, derive the estimates maximizing over parameters and missing data in Eq. (6.71).
6.21 For Example 6.28, derive the estimates maximizing over parameters and missing data, as described in the example.
6.22 In Example 6.29, derive the estimates maximizing over parameters and missing data.

## Notes

1 A technically more precise formulation is to write $A^{-1}(\theta-\hat{\theta}) \sim N\left(0, I_{d}\right)$, where $A$ is the matrix square root of $C$ (that is, $A^{\mathrm{T}} A=A A^{\mathrm{T}}=C$ ) and $I_{d}$ is the ( $d \times d$ ) identity matrix.
2 More formally, $y_{(0)}$ is an ( $n \times K$ ) matrix with entries $y_{i j}$ when $m_{i j}=0$ and * when $m_{i j}=1$. In earlier editions, we used the notation $y_{\text {obs }}$ and $y_{\text {mis }}$ for $y_{(0)}$ and $y_{(1)}$, respectively. This notation has led to some confusion, as discussed in Seaman et al. (2013) and Mealli and Rubin (2015), and here, we revert a notation closer to the original notation in Rubin (1976a).
3 In earlier work (Rubin 1976a; Little and Rubin 1987), the term "distinctness" was used in the Bayesian setting to refer to a priori independence, but here, we simply label the condition as "a priori independence."

## Factored Likelihood Methods When the Missingness Mechanism Is Ignorable

### 7.1 Introduction

We now assume that the missingness mechanism is ignorable, and for simplicity, write $\ell\left(\theta \mid y_{(0)}\right)$ for the ignorable loglikelihood $\ell_{\text {ign }}\left(\theta \mid y_{(0)}\right)$ based on the observed data $y_{(0)}$. This can be a complicated function with no obvious maximum and an apparently complex form for the information matrix. Analogous issues arise with simulating from the resulting posterior distributions without simple structures. For certain models and incomplete data patterns, however, analyses based on $\ell\left(\theta \mid y_{(0)}\right)$ can employ standard complete-data techniques. The general idea will be described here in this section, and then, specific examples will be given in the remainder of this chapter for normal data and in Section 12.2 for multinomial (that is, cross-classified) data.

For a variety of models and missingness patterns, an alternative parameterization $\phi=\phi(\theta)$, where $\phi$ is a one-to-one function of $\theta$, can be found such that the loglikelihood decomposes into $J$ terms

$$
\ell\left(\phi \mid y_{(0)}\right)=\ell_{1}\left(\phi_{1} \mid y_{(0)}\right)+\ell_{2}\left(\phi_{2} \mid y_{(0)}\right)+\cdots+\ell_{J}\left(\phi_{J} \mid y_{(0)}\right)
$$

where

1. $\phi_{1}, \phi_{2}, \ldots, \phi_{J}$ are distinct parameters, in the sense that the joint parameter space of $\phi=\left(\phi_{1}, \phi_{2}, \ldots, \phi_{J}\right)$ is the product of the individual parameter spaces for $\phi_{j}, j=1, \ldots, J$; and
2. each component $\ell_{j}\left(\phi_{j} \mid y_{(0)}\right)$ corresponds to a loglikelihood for a completedata problem, or more generally, for an incomplete-data problem that is easier to analyze than that based on $\ell\left(\theta \mid y_{(0)}\right)$.

For a Bayesian analysis with a prior distribution specified for the parameters, the aforementioned condition (1) is replaced by the requirement that $\phi_{1}, \phi_{2}, \ldots, \phi_{J}$ are mutually a priori independent.

If a factored likelihood with these properties can be found, then $\ell\left(\phi \mid y_{(0)}\right)$ can be maximized by maximizing $\ell_{j}\left(\phi_{j} \mid y_{(0)}\right)$ separately for each $j$. If $\hat{\phi}$ is the resulting maximum likelihood (ML) estimate of $\phi$, then the ML estimate of any function $\theta(\phi)$ of $\phi$ is obtained by applying Property 6.1, that is, substituting $\hat{\theta}=\theta(\hat{\phi})$. Similarly, for Bayesian inference, when (1) and (2) hold, the posterior distribution of $\phi$ is the product of $J$ independent posterior distributions of $\phi_{1}, \phi_{2}, \ldots, \phi_{J}$, and hence, often has a much simpler form than the posterior distribution of $\theta$. The posterior distribution of $\theta$ can be simulated by generating draws $\left\{\phi^{(d)}, d=1, \ldots, D\right\}$ from the posterior distribution of $\phi$ and then computing $\left\{\theta^{(d)}=\theta\left(\phi^{(d)}\right), d=1, \ldots, D\right\}$, which is a set of draws from the posterior distribution of $\theta$ by Property 6.1B.

The decomposition (7.1) can also be used to calculate the large-sample covariance matrix associated with the ML estimates, as given in Approximations 6.1 and 6.2. Differentiating (7.1) twice with respect to $\phi_{1}, \ldots, \phi_{J}$ yields a block-diagonal information matrix for $\phi$ of the form

$$
I\left(\phi \mid y_{(0)}\right)=\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
I\left(\phi_{1} \mid y_{(0)}\right) & & & 0 \\
& I\left(\phi_{2} \mid y_{(0)}\right) & & \\
& & \ddots & \\
0 & & & I\left(\phi_{J} \mid y_{(0)}\right)
\end{array}\right]
$$

Hence, the large sample covariance matrix for $\phi-\hat{\phi}$ is also block diagonal, with the form

$$
C\left(\phi-\hat{\phi} \mid y_{(0)}\right)=\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
I^{-1}\left(\hat{\phi}_{1} \mid y_{(0)}\right) & & & 0 \\
& I^{-1}\left(\hat{\phi}_{2} \mid y_{(0)}\right) & & \\
& & \ddots & \\
0 & & & I^{-1}\left(\hat{\phi}_{J} \mid y_{(0)}\right)
\end{array}\right]
$$

Because each nonzero component of this matrix corresponds to a completedata problem, each is often relatively easy to calculate. By Property 6.2, the approximate large-sample covariance matrix of the ML estimate of a function $\theta=\theta(\phi)$ of $\phi$ can be found using the formula

$$
C\left(\theta-\hat{\theta} \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}\right)=D(\hat{\theta}) C\left(\hat{\phi} \mid \tilde{y}_{(0)}\right) D^{\mathrm{T}}(\hat{\theta})
$$

where $D(\cdot)$ is the matrix of partial derivatives of the column vector $\theta$ with respect to $\phi$ :

$$
D(\theta)=\left\{d_{j k}(\theta)\right\}, \quad \text { where } d_{j k}(\theta)=\frac{\partial \theta_{j}}{\partial \phi_{k}}
$$

### 7.2 Bivariate Normal Data with One Variable Subject to Missingness: ML Estimation

### 7.2.1 ML Estimates

Anderson (1957) first introduced factored likelihoods for the normal data of Example 6.24.

Example 7.1 Bivariate Normal Sample with One Variable Subject to Nonresponse (Example 6.24 Continued). The loglikelihood for a bivariate normal sample with $r$ complete bivariate units $\left\{\left(y_{i 1}, y_{i 2}\right), i=1, \ldots, r\right\}$ and $n-r$ univariate units $\left\{y_{i 1}, i=r+1, \ldots, n\right\}$ is given by (6.52). ML estimates of $\mu$ and $\Sigma$ are found by maximizing this function with respect to $\mu$ and $\Sigma$. The likelihood equations, however, do not have an obvious solution. Anderson (1957) factors the joint distribution of $y_{i 1}$ and $y_{i 2}$ into the marginal distribution of $y_{i 1}$ and the conditional distribution of $y_{i 2}$ given $y_{i 1}$ :

$$
f_{Y}\left(y_{i 1}, y_{i 2} \mid \mu, \Sigma\right)=f_{1}\left(y_{i 1} \mid \mu_{1}, \sigma_{11}\right) f_{2}\left(y_{i 2} \mid y_{i 1}, \beta_{20 \cdot 1}, \beta_{21 \cdot 1}, \sigma_{22 \cdot 1}\right),
$$

where, by properties of the bivariate normal distribution discussed in Example 6.9, $f_{1}\left(y_{i 1} \mid \mu_{1}, \sigma_{11}\right)$ is the univariate normal distribution with mean $\mu_{1}$ and variance $\sigma_{11}$, and $f_{2}\left(y_{i 2} \mid y_{i 1}, \beta_{20 \cdot 1}, \beta_{21 \cdot 1}, \sigma_{22 \cdot 1}\right)$ is the univariate conditional normal distribution with mean

$$
\beta_{20 \cdot 1}+\beta_{21 \cdot 1} y_{i 1}
$$

and variance $\sigma_{22 \cdot 1}$. The parameter

$$
\phi=\left(\mu_{1}, \sigma_{11}, \beta_{20 \cdot 1}, \beta_{21 \cdot 1}, \sigma_{22 \cdot 1}\right)^{\mathrm{T}}
$$

is a one-to-one function of the original parameter

$$
\theta=\left(\mu_{1}, \mu_{2}, \sigma_{11}, \sigma_{12}, \sigma_{22}\right)^{\mathrm{T}}
$$

of the joint distribution of $y_{i 1}$ and $y_{i 2}$. In particular, $\mu_{1}$ and $\sigma_{11}$ are common to both parameterizations, and the other components of $\phi$ are given by the following functions of components of $\theta$ :

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta_{21 \cdot 1} & =\sigma_{12} / \sigma_{11}, \\
\beta_{20 \cdot 1} & =\mu_{2}-\beta_{21 \cdot 1} \mu_{1}, \\
\sigma_{22 \cdot 1} & =\sigma_{22}-\sigma_{12}^{2} / \sigma_{11} .
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly, the components of $\theta$ other than $\mu_{1}$ and $\sigma_{11}$ can be expressed as the following functions of the components of $\phi$ :

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{2} & =\beta_{20 \cdot 1}+\beta_{21 \cdot 1} \mu_{1}, \\
\sigma_{12} & =\beta_{21 \cdot 1} \sigma_{11}, \\
\sigma_{22} & =\sigma_{22 \cdot 1}+\beta_{21 \cdot 1}^{2} \sigma_{11} .
\end{aligned}
$$

The density of the data $y_{(0)}$ factors in the following way:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f\left(y_{(0)} \mid \theta\right) & =\prod_{i=1}^{r} f_{Y}\left(y_{i 1}, y_{i 2} \mid \theta\right) \prod_{i=r+1}^{n} f_{1}\left(y_{i 1} \mid \theta\right) \\
& =\left[\prod_{i=1}^{r} f_{1}\left(y_{i 1} \mid \theta\right) f_{2}\left(y_{i 2} \mid y_{i 1}, \theta\right)\right]\left[\prod_{i=r+1}^{n} f_{1}\left(y_{i 1} \mid \theta\right)\right] \\
& =\left[\prod_{i=1}^{n} f_{1}\left(y_{i 1} \mid \mu_{1}, \sigma_{11}\right)\right]\left[\prod_{i=1}^{r} f_{2}\left(y_{i 2} \mid y_{i 1}, \beta_{20 \cdot 1}, \beta_{21 \cdot 1}, \sigma_{22 \cdot 1}\right)\right] .
\end{aligned}
$$

The first bracketed factor in the last row of (7.6) is the density of an independent and identically distributed sample of size $n$ from the normal distribution with mean $\mu_{1}$ and variance $\sigma_{11}$. The second factor is the density for $r$ independent units from the conditional normal distribution with mean $\beta_{20 \cdot 1}+\beta_{21 \cdot 1} y_{i 1}$ and variance $\sigma_{22 \cdot 1}$. Furthermore, if the parameter space for $\theta$ is the standard natural parameter space with no prior restrictions, then ( $\mu_{1}, \sigma_{11}$ ) and ( $\beta_{20 \cdot 1}$, $\beta_{21 \cdot 1}, \sigma_{22 \cdot 1}$ ) are distinct, implying that knowledge about ( $\mu_{1}, \sigma_{11}$ ) does not provide any knowledge about $\left(\beta_{20 \cdot 1}, \beta_{21 \cdot 1}, \sigma_{22 \cdot 1}\right)$. Hence, ML estimates of $\phi$ can be obtained by independently maximizing the likelihoods corresponding to these two sets of components.

Maximizing the first factor yields

$$
\hat{\mu}_{1}=n^{-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_{i 1}, \quad \hat{\sigma}_{11}=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_{i 1}-\hat{\mu}_{1}\right)^{2} / n
$$

Maximizing the second factor uses standard regression results and yields estimates from the $r$ complete units (cf. Example 6.9):

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1}=\tilde{\sigma}_{12} / \tilde{\sigma}_{11} \\
& \hat{\beta}_{20 \cdot 1}=\bar{y}_{2}-\hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1} \bar{y}_{1} \\
& \hat{\sigma}_{22 \cdot 1}=\tilde{\sigma}_{22 \cdot 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\bar{y}_{j}=r^{-1} \sum_{i=1}^{r} y_{i j}, \tilde{\sigma}_{j k}=\sum_{i=1}^{r}\left(y_{i j}-\bar{y}_{j}\right)\left(y_{i k}-\bar{y}_{k}\right) / r$ for $j, k=1,2$ and $\tilde{\sigma}_{22 \cdot 1}=\tilde{\sigma}_{22}-\tilde{\sigma}_{12}^{2} / \tilde{\sigma}_{11}$ are the ML estimates of their respective parameters if only the first $r$ units were observed. The ML estimates of other parameters can now be obtained using Property 6.1. In particular, from (7.5),

$$
\hat{\mu}_{2}=\hat{\beta}_{20 \cdot 1}+\hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1} \hat{\mu}_{1},
$$

or from (7.7) and (7.8),

$$
\hat{\mu}_{2}=\bar{y}_{2}+\hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1}\left(\hat{\mu}_{1}-\bar{y}_{1}\right) ;
$$

from (7.5),

$$
\hat{\sigma}_{22}=\tilde{\sigma}_{22 \cdot 1}+\hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1}^{2} \hat{\sigma}_{11},
$$

or from (7.7) and (7.8),

$$
\hat{\sigma}_{22}=\tilde{\sigma}_{22}+\hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1}^{2}\left(\hat{\sigma}_{11}-\tilde{\sigma}_{11}\right) .
$$

Finally, from (7.5), we have for the correlation

$$
\rho \equiv \sigma_{12}\left(\sigma_{11} \sigma_{22}\right)^{-1 / 2}=\beta_{21 \cdot 1} \sigma_{11}^{1 / 2}\left(\sigma_{22 \cdot 1}+\beta_{21 \cdot 1}^{2} \sigma_{11}\right)^{-1 / 2}
$$

so from (7.7) and (7.8),

$$
\hat{\rho}=\tilde{\rho}\left(\hat{\sigma}_{11} / \tilde{\sigma}_{11}\right)^{1 / 2}\left(\tilde{\sigma}_{22} / \hat{\sigma}_{22}\right)^{1 / 2}
$$

where $\tilde{\rho}=\tilde{\sigma}_{12}\left(\tilde{\sigma}_{11} \tilde{\sigma}_{22}\right)^{-1 / 2}$. The first terms on the right side of (7.9) and (7.10) and the first factor on the right side of (7.11) are the ML estimates of $\mu_{2}, \sigma_{22}$, and $\rho$ with the $n-r$ incomplete units discarded. The remaining terms and factors in these expressions represent adjustments based on the additional information from the $n-r$ extra values of $y_{i 1}$.

The ML estimate (7.9) of the mean of $y_{i 2}$ is of particular interest. It can be written in the form

$$
\hat{\mu}_{2}=n^{-1}\left\{\sum_{i=1}^{r} y_{i 2}+\sum_{i=r+1}^{n} \hat{y}_{i 2}\right\},
$$

where

$$
\hat{y}_{i 2}=\bar{y}_{2}+\hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1}\left(y_{i 1}-\bar{y}_{1}\right) .
$$

Hence, $\hat{\mu}_{2}$ is a type of regression estimator commonly used in sample surveys (e.g., Cochran 1977), which effectively imputes the predicted values $\hat{y}_{i 2}$ for missing $y_{i 2}$ from the linear regression of observed $y_{i 2}$ on observed $y_{i 1}$.

Example 7.2 Bivariate Normal Numerical Illustration. The first $r=12$ units in Table 7.1, taken from Snedecor and Cochran (1967, table 6.9.1), give measurements on the size of crop from apple trees, in hundreds of fruits $\left(y_{i 1}\right)$ and 100 times the percentage of wormy fruits $\left(y_{i 2}\right)$. These units suggest a negative association between the size of crop and the percentage of wormy fruits. Suppose that the objective is to estimate the mean of $y_{i 2}$, but the value of $y_{i 2}$ was not determined for some of the trees with smaller crops, numbered $13-18$ in the table. The sample mean for the complete units, $\bar{y}_{2}=45$, underestimates the

Table 7.1 Example 7.2: data on size of apple crop ( $y_{i 1}$ ) and $100 \times$ percentage of wormy fruit ( $y_{i 2}$ )
| Tree number | Size of crop (100s of fruits) ( $y_{i 1}$ ) | $100 \times$ percentage wormy fruits ( $y_{i 2}$ ) | Regression prediction ( $y_{i 2}$ ) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 8 | 59 | 56.1 |
| 2 | 6 | 58 | 58.2 |
| 3 | 11 | 56 | 53.1 |
| 4 | 22 | 53 | 42.0 |
| 5 | 14 | 50 | 50.1 |
| 6 | 17 | 45 | 47.0 |
| 7 | 18 | 43 | 46.0 |
| 8 | 24 | 42 | 39.9 |
| 9 | 19 | 39 | 45.0 |
| 10 | 23 | 38 | 41.0 |
| 11 | 26 | 30 | 37.9 |
| 12 | 40 | 27 | 23.7 |
| 13 | 4 | ? | 60.2 |
| 14 | 4 | ? | 60.2 |
| 15 | 5 | ? | 59.2 |
| 16 | 6 | ? | 58.2 |
| 17 | 8 | ? | 56.1 |
| 18 | 10 | ? | 54.1 |
| $\bar{y}_{1}=19 ; \quad \bar{y}_{2}=45 ; \quad \hat{\mu}_{2}=49.3333 ; \quad \hat{\mu}_{1}=14.7222$ |  |  |  |
| $\tilde{\sigma}_{11}=77.0 ; \quad \tilde{\sigma}_{12}=-78.0 ; \quad \tilde{\sigma}_{22}=101.8333 ; \quad \hat{\sigma}_{11}=89.5340$ |  |  |  |


? denotes missing.
Source: Snedecor and Cochran (1967, table 6.9.1). Adapted with permission of Iowa State University Press.
percentage of wormy fruits because the percentage for the six omitted trees, which tend to be smaller, is expected to be larger than the percentage for the measured trees (that is the data may be missing at random [MAR] but do not appear to be missing completely at random [MCAR]). The ML estimate assuming ignorability (that is MAR and distinctness of the data and missingness parameters) is $\hat{\mu}_{2}=49.33$, which can be compared with the estimate $\bar{y}_{2}=45$ from the complete units. This analysis should be taken only as a numerical illustration; a serious analysis of these data would consider issues such as whether transformations of $y_{i 1}$ and $y_{i 2}$ (e.g., log, square root) would better meet the underlying linearity and normality assumptions.

### 7.2.2 Large-Sample Covariance Matrix

The large-sample covariance matrix of ( $\phi-\hat{\phi}$ ) is found by calculating and inverting the information matrix. The loglikelihood of $\phi$ is, from Eq. (7.6),

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell\left(\phi \mid y_{(0)}\right)= & -\left(2 \sigma_{22 \cdot 1}\right)^{-1} \sum_{i=1}^{r}\left(y_{i 2}-\beta_{20 \cdot 1}-\beta_{21 \cdot 1} y_{i 1}\right)^{2}-\frac{1}{2} r \ln \sigma_{22 \cdot 1} \\
& -\left(2 \sigma_{11}\right)^{-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_{i 1}-\mu_{1}\right)^{2}-\frac{1}{2} n \ln \sigma_{11}
\end{aligned}
$$

Differentiating twice with respect to $\phi$ gives

$$
I\left(\hat{\phi} \mid y_{(0)}\right)=\left[\begin{array}{cc}
I_{11}\left(\hat{\mu}_{1}, \hat{\sigma}_{11} \mid y_{(0)}\right) & 0 \\
0 & I_{22}\left(\hat{\beta}_{20 \cdot 1}, \hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1}, \hat{\sigma}_{22 \cdot 1} \mid y_{(0)}\right)
\end{array}\right]
$$

where

$$
I_{11}\left(\hat{\mu}_{1}, \hat{\sigma}_{11} \mid y_{(0)}\right)=\left[\begin{array}{cc}
n / \hat{\sigma}_{11} & 0 \\
0 & n /\left(2 \hat{\sigma}_{11}^{2}\right)
\end{array}\right]
$$

and

$$
I_{22}\left(\hat{\beta}_{20 \cdot 1}, \hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1}, \hat{\sigma}_{22 \cdot 1} \mid y_{(0)}\right)=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
r / \hat{\sigma}_{22 \cdot 1} & r \bar{y}_{1} / \hat{\sigma}_{22 \cdot 1} & 0 \\
r \bar{y}_{1} / \hat{\sigma}_{22 \cdot 1} & \sum_{i=1}^{r} y_{i 1}^{2} / \hat{\sigma}_{22 \cdot 1} & 0 \\
0 & 0 & r /\left(2 \hat{\sigma}_{22 \cdot 1}^{2}\right)
\end{array}\right]
$$

Inverting these matrices yields the large-sample covariance matrix of $(\phi-\hat{\phi})$ as follows:

$$
C(\phi-\hat{\phi})=\left[\begin{array}{cc}
I_{11}^{-1}\left(\hat{\mu}_{1}, \hat{\sigma}_{11} \mid y_{(0)}\right) & 0 \\
0 & I_{22}^{-1}\left(\hat{\beta}_{20 \cdot 1}, \hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1}, \hat{\sigma}_{22 \cdot 1} \mid y_{(0)}\right)
\end{array}\right]
$$

where

$$
I_{11}^{-1}\left(\hat{\mu}_{1}, \hat{\sigma}_{11} \mid y_{(0)}\right)=\left[\begin{array}{cc}
\hat{\sigma}_{11} / n & 0 \\
0 & 2 \hat{\sigma}_{11}^{2} / n
\end{array}\right]
$$

and
$I_{22}^{-1}\left(\hat{\beta}_{20 \cdot 1}, \hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1}, \hat{\sigma}_{22 \cdot 1} \mid y_{(0)}\right)=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{\sigma}_{22 \cdot 1}\left(1+\bar{y}_{1}^{2} / \tilde{\sigma}_{11}\right) / r & -\bar{y}_{1} \hat{\sigma}_{22 \cdot 1} /\left(r \tilde{\sigma}_{11}\right) & 0 \\ -\bar{y}_{1} \hat{\sigma}_{22 \cdot 1} /\left(r \tilde{\sigma}_{11}\right) & \hat{\sigma}_{22 \cdot 1} /\left(r s \tilde{\sigma}_{11}\right) & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \hat{\sigma}_{22 \cdot 1}^{2} / r\end{array}\right]$.

The large sample covariance matrix of $(\theta-\hat{\theta})$ can be found using Eq. (7.3). To illustrate the calculations, we consider the parameter $\mu_{2}$, the mean of the incompletely observed variable. Because $\mu_{2}=\beta_{20 \cdot 1}+\beta_{21 \cdot 1} \mu_{1}$, we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
D\left(\mu_{2}\right) & =\left(\frac{\partial \mu_{2}}{\partial \mu_{1}}, \frac{\partial \mu_{2}}{\partial \sigma_{11}}, \frac{\partial \mu_{2}}{\partial \beta_{20 \cdot 1}}, \frac{\partial \mu_{2}}{\partial \beta_{21 \cdot 1}}, \frac{\partial \mu_{2}}{\partial \sigma_{22 \cdot 1}}\right) \\
& =\left(\hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1}, 0,1, \hat{\mu}_{1}, 0\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

substituting ML estimates of $\mu_{1}$ and $\beta_{21 \cdot 1}$. Hence, with some calculation, the large-sample variance of ( $\mu_{2}-\hat{\mu}_{2}$ ) is

$$
\operatorname{Var}\left(\mu_{2}-\hat{\mu}_{2}\right)=D\left(\hat{\mu}_{2}\right) C(\phi-\hat{\phi}) D\left(\hat{\mu}_{2}\right)^{\mathrm{T}}=\hat{\sigma}_{22 \cdot 1}\left[\frac{1}{r}+\frac{\hat{\rho}^{2}}{n\left(1-\hat{\rho}^{2}\right)}+\frac{\left(\bar{y}_{1}-\hat{\mu}_{1}\right)^{2}}{r \tilde{\sigma}_{11}}\right]
$$

The third term in the brackets is of order ( $1 / r^{2}$ ) if the data are MCAR because in that case, $\left(\bar{y}_{1}-\hat{\mu}_{1}\right)^{2}$ is of order $(1 / r)$. Ignoring this term yields

$$
\operatorname{Var}\left(\mu_{2}-\hat{\mu}_{2}\right) \approx \hat{\sigma}_{22 \cdot 1}\left[\frac{1}{r}+\frac{\hat{\rho}^{2}}{n\left(1-\hat{\rho}^{2}\right)}\right]=\frac{\hat{\sigma}_{22}}{r}\left(1-\hat{\rho}^{2} \frac{n-r}{n}\right)
$$

which can be compared with the sampling variance of $\bar{y}_{2}$, namely $\sigma_{22} / r$. Thus, in large samples, under MCAR, the proportionate reduction in sampling variance obtained by including the $n-r$ units with only $y_{1}$ observed is $\rho^{2}$ times the fraction of incomplete units $(n-r) / n$.

### 7.3 Bivariate Normal Monotone Data: Small-Sample Inference

Given large samples, interval estimates for the parameters can be obtained by applying Approximation 6.1 (Eq. (6.18)), as discussed in Section 6.1.3. In particular, an asymptotic $95 \%$ interval for $\mu_{2}$ takes the form

$$
\hat{\mu}_{2} \pm 1.96 \sqrt{\operatorname{Var}\left(\hat{\mu}_{2}-\mu_{2}\right)}
$$

where $\operatorname{Var}\left(\hat{\mu}_{2}-\mu_{2}\right)$ is approximated by Eq. (7.13). For parameters other than means or regression coefficients, better intervals are generally obtained by applying a transformation to approximate normality, calculating a normalbased interval for the transformed parameter, and then transforming the interval back to the original scale (see Property 6.2 and Approximation 6.2 in Section 6.1.3). For example, an appropriate transformation for a variance



parameter is the logarithm, so to compute a $95 \%$ interval for $\sigma_{22}$, a $95 \%$ interval for $\ell n\left(\sigma_{22}\right)$ is computed as

$$
\ell n\left(\hat{\sigma}_{22}\right) \pm 1.96 \sqrt{\operatorname{Var}\left(\ell n\left(\hat{\sigma}_{22}\right)-\ell n\left(\sigma_{22}\right)\right)},
$$

where in large samples, $\operatorname{Var}\left(\ell n\left(\hat{\sigma}_{22}\right)-\ell n\left(\sigma_{22}\right)\right)=\operatorname{Var}\left(\hat{\sigma}_{22}-\sigma_{22}\right) / \hat{\sigma}_{22}^{2}$. The $95 \%$ interval for $\hat{\sigma}_{22}$ is then $(\exp (l), \exp (u))$ where $(l, u)$ is the interval for $\ell\left(\hat{\sigma}_{22}\right)$ computed from (7.16).

Small-sample inference is problematic from a frequentist perspective. In particular, the quantity $\left(\hat{\mu}_{2}-\mu_{2}\right) / \sqrt{\operatorname{Var}\left(\hat{\mu}_{2}-\mu_{2}\right)}$ obtained from (7.13) is standard normally distributed in large samples, but its distribution in small samples is complex and depends on the parameters. The $t$ distribution with $r-1$ degrees of freedom has been suggested as a useful approximate reference distribution for this quantity and performs reasonably well in simulations (Little 1976). The same reference $t$ distribution has also been proposed for inference about the difference in means $\mu_{2}-\mu_{1}$, based on $\left(\hat{\mu}_{2}-\hat{\mu}_{1}-\mu_{2}+\mu_{1}\right) / \sqrt{\operatorname{Var}\left(\hat{\mu}_{2}-\hat{\mu}_{1}-\mu_{2}+\mu_{1}\right)}$.

A more direct (and in our view, more principled) approach to small-sample interval estimation is to specify a prior distribution for the parameters and then derive the associated posterior distribution. Specifically, suppose $\mu_{1}, \sigma_{11}, \beta_{20 \cdot 1}$, $\beta_{21 \cdot 1}$, and $\sigma_{22 \cdot 1}$ are assumed a priori independent with convenience prior

$$
f\left(\mu_{1}, \sigma_{11}, \beta_{20 \cdot 1}, \beta_{21 \cdot 1}, \sigma_{22 \cdot 1}\right) \propto \sigma_{11}^{-a} \sigma_{22 \cdot 1}^{-c}
$$

The choice $a=c=1$ yields the Jeffreys' prior for the factored density (Box and Tiao 1973).

Applying standard Bayesian theory to the random sample $\left\{y_{i 1}: i=1, \ldots, n\right\}$, we have the following results: (i) the posterior distribution of ( $\mu_{1}, \sigma_{11}$ ) is such that $n \hat{\sigma}_{11} / \sigma_{11}$ has a chi-squared distribution with $n+2 a-3$ degrees of freedom, and (ii) the posterior distribution of $\mu_{1}$ given $\sigma_{11}$ is normal with mean $\hat{\mu}_{1}$ and variance $\sigma_{11} / n$; applying standard Bayesian regression theory to the random sample $\left\{\left(y_{i 1}, y_{i 2}\right): i=1, \ldots, r\right\}$, (iii) the posterior distribution of $\left(\beta_{20 \cdot 1}, \beta_{21 \cdot 1}, \sigma_{22 \cdot 1}\right)$ is such that $r \hat{\sigma}_{22 \cdot 1} / \sigma_{22 \cdot 1}$ has a chi-squared distribution with $r+2 c-4$ degrees of freedom, (iv) the posterior distribution of $\beta_{21 \cdot 1}$ given $\sigma_{22 \cdot 1}$ is normal with mean $\hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1}$ and variance $\sigma_{22 \cdot 1} /\left(r \tilde{\sigma}_{11}\right)$, and (v) the posterior distribution of $\beta_{20 \cdot 1}$ given $\beta_{21 \cdot 1}$ and $\sigma_{22 \cdot 1}$ is normal with mean $\bar{y}_{2}-\beta_{21 \cdot 1} \bar{y}_{1}$ and variance $\sigma_{22 \cdot 1} / r$; furthermore, (vi) ( $\mu_{1}, \sigma_{11}$ ) and ( $\beta_{20 \cdot 1}, \beta_{21 \cdot 1}, \sigma_{22 \cdot 1}$ ) are a posteriori independent. For derivations of these results, see for example Lindley (1965) or Gelman et al. (2013).

Results 1-6 and Property 6.1B imply that the posterior distribution of any function of the parameters $\phi$ can be simulated by creating $D$ draws, $d=1, \ldots, D$, as follows:

1. Draw independently $x_{1 t}^{2}$ and $x_{2 t}^{2}$ from chi-squared distributions with $n+2 a-3$ and $r+2 c-4$ degrees of freedom, respectively. Draw three independent standard normal deviates $z_{1 t}, z_{2 t}$, and $z_{3 t}$.
2. Compute $\phi^{(d)}=\left(\sigma_{11}^{(d)}, \mu_{1}^{(d)}, \sigma_{22 \cdot 1}^{(d)}, \beta_{20 \cdot 1}^{(d)}, \beta_{21 \cdot 1}^{(d)}\right)^{\mathrm{T}}$, where

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{11}^{(d)} & =n \hat{\sigma}_{11} / x_{1 t}^{2} \\
\mu_{1}^{(d)} & =\hat{\mu}_{1}+z_{1 t}\left(\sigma_{11}^{(d)} / n\right)^{1 / 2} \\
\sigma_{22 \cdot 1}^{(d)} & =r \hat{\sigma}_{22 \cdot 1} / x_{2 t}^{2} \\
\beta_{21 \cdot 1}^{(d)} & =\hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1}+z_{2 t}\left(\sigma_{22 \cdot 1}^{(d)} /\left(r \tilde{\sigma}_{11}\right)\right)^{1 / 2} \\
\beta_{20 \cdot 1}^{(d)} & =\bar{y}_{2}-\beta_{21 \cdot 1}^{(d)} \bar{y}_{1}+z_{3 t}\left(\sigma_{22 \cdot 1}^{(d)} / r\right)^{1 / 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

3. Compute the corresponding transformation of $\phi^{(d)}$. For example, if the transformation is $\mu_{2}=\beta_{20 \cdot 1}+\beta_{21 \cdot 1} \mu_{1}$, then $\mu_{2}^{(d)}=\beta_{20 \cdot 1}^{(d)}+\beta_{21 \cdot 1}^{(d)} \mu_{1}^{(d)}$.
The methods of the previous two sections are now applied to the data in Table 7.1.

Example 7.3 Bayes Interval Estimation for the Bivariate Normal (Example 7.2 Continued). Table 7.2 shows $95 \%$ intervals for $\mu_{2}, \sigma_{22}$, and $\rho$ for the data in Table 7.1. Intervals based on four methods are presented:
(1) Asymptotic intervals based on the inverse of the observed information matrix of $\left(\mu_{2}, \sigma_{22}, \rho\right)$ (e.g., Eq. (7.15) for $\mu_{2}$ );
(2) Intervals with standard errors based on the inverse of the observed information matrix of $\left(\mu_{2}, \ln \sigma_{22}, Z_{\rho}\right)$, where $Z_{\rho}=\ln [(1+\rho) /(1-\rho)] / 2$ is Fisher's

Table 7.2 Example 7.3: 95\% intervals for parameters of bivariate normal distribution, based on data in Table 7.1
| Method |  | Parameters |  |  |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
|  |  | $\mu_{2}$ | $\sigma_{22}$ | $\rho$ |
| (1) | Asymptotic theory | (44.0, 54.7) | (30.7, 198.7) | (-1.00, -0.79) |
| (2) | Asymptotic theory, with transform and $t$ approximation | (43.4, 55.3) | (50.8, 258.9) | (-0.97, -0.69) |
| (3) | Bayes simulation A | (43.7, 54.4) | (60.1, 289.7) | (-0.96, -0.66) |
| (3) | Bayes simulation B | (43.5, 55.7) | (59.2, 293.9) | (-0.96, -0.66) |
| (4) | Normal approximation to A | (43.5, 55.2) | (15.9, 256.7) | (-1.03, -0.72) |
| (4) | Normal approximation to B | (43.4, 55.2) | (14.6, 257.2) | (-1.03, -0.71) |
| ML estimates |  | 49.33 | 114.70 | -0.895 |


normalizing transformation of the correlation. The normal percentile 1.96 of the asymptotic normal interval is replaced by the 97.5th percentile (2.201) of the $t$ distribution on $r-1=11$ degrees of freedom.
(3) The 2.5th to 97.5th percentile of the Bayesian posterior distribution with prior distribution (7.17) with $a=c=1$, simulated using the method of Section 7.3 with 9999 simulated values; and
(4) Intervals obtained by fitting normal distributions to the simulated posterior distributions from method (3), using the mean and variance of the 9999 simulated values.

Methods 3 and 4 are repeated for two independent sets of random numbers (A and B) to give some idea of the simulation variance.

The asymptotic interval for $\mu_{2}$ from method (1) is shorter than for the other methods, and presumably has lower than the stated $95 \%$ coverage, because uncertainty due to estimation of the variance parameters is not taken into account. The other intervals for $\mu_{2}$ are fairly similar to each other. The intervals for $\sigma_{22}$ and $\rho$ that rely on normality on the original scale (methods (1) and (4)) are not satisfactory - in particular, the lower limits of the intervals for the correlation lie outside the parameter space. The intervals for methods (2) and (3) are broadly similar. Method (2) forces symmetry around the ML estimates of $\ln \sigma_{22}$ and $Z_{\rho}$, and method (4) forces symmetry about the sample mean of the posterior draws of $\sigma_{22}$ and $\rho$ : the normal approximations of method (4) should be applied to the draws on the transformed scale and then the interval transformed back to the original scale. Method (3) has the advantage of not imposing these symmetries on the intervals but suffers from simulation error from the finite number of posterior draws, $D=9999$; this is a minor issue and easily corrected by increasing $D$. Coverage properties of these intervals in repeated sampling deserve more extensive simulation; for example, Little (1988a) studies coverage properties of various $t$ approximations to the posterior distribution of $\mu_{2}$, but current computing environments make more extensive evaluations straightforward.

### 7.4 Monotone Missingness with More Than Two Variables

### 7.4.1 Multivariate Data with One Normal Variable Subject to Missingness

A simple but important extension of bivariate monotone missing data is given in the next example.

Example 7.4 $K+1$ Variables, One Subject to Missingness. Suppose we replace $y_{i 1}$ by a set of $K$ completely observed variables, as for the data pattern in Figure 1.1a, resulting in a special case of monotone data with $J=2$ and $Y_{1}$ representing $K$ variables. Suppose first that $\left(y_{i 1}, y_{i 2}\right)$ are iid $(K+1)$-variate normally
distributed, and the data are MAR with distinct parameters. The ML estimates of $\mu_{2}$ and $\sigma_{22}$ are then

$$
\hat{\mu}_{2}=\bar{y}_{2}+\left(\hat{\mu}_{1}-\bar{y}_{1}\right)^{\mathrm{T}} \hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1}
$$

and

$$
\hat{\sigma}_{22}=s_{22}+\hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1}^{\mathrm{T}}\left(\hat{\sigma}_{11}-\tilde{\sigma}_{11}\right) \hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1}
$$

where $\hat{\mu}_{1}$ and $\bar{y}_{1}$ are $(K \times 1)$ vectors, $\hat{\mu}_{1}$ the mean of $y_{i 1}$ for all $n$ units, $\bar{y}_{1}$ the sample mean of $y_{i 1}$ for the $r$ complete units, respectively, $\hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1}$ is the $(K \times 1)$ vector of estimated regression coefficients from the multiple regression of $y_{i 2}$ on $y_{i 1}$, and $\hat{\sigma}_{11}$ and $\tilde{\sigma}_{11}$ are the ( $K \times K$ ) sample covariance matrices, $\hat{\sigma}_{11}$ based on all $n$ units of $y_{i 1}$ and $\tilde{\sigma}_{11}$ based on the $r$ units of $y_{i 1}$ where $y_{i 2}$ is also observed. The ML estimate $\hat{\mu}_{2}$ corresponds to imputing the missing values of $y_{i 2}$ using the ML estimates of the parameters of the multiple regression of $y_{i 2}$ on $y_{i 1}$.

More generally, (7.18) is also ML for $\hat{\mu}_{2}$ if the data are MAR and

1. $y_{i 2}$ given $y_{i 1}$ is normal with mean ( $\beta_{20 \cdot 1}+y_{i 1} \beta_{21 \cdot 1}$ ) and variance $\sigma_{22 \cdot 1}$.
2. $y_{i 1}$ has any distribution such that (i) $\hat{\mu}_{1}$ is the ML estimate of the mean of $y_{i 1}$, and (ii) $\mu_{1}$ and $\beta_{20 \cdot 1}, \beta_{21 \cdot 1}, \sigma_{22 \cdot 1}$ are distinct from the parameters of the distribution of $y_{i 1}$.

A special case is dummy variable regression, where $y_{i 1}$ represents $K$ dummy variables indicating $K+1$ groups. The $k$ th component of $y_{i 1}$ is defined to be 1 if the $i$ th unit belongs to group $k$ and is zero otherwise: For units in group 1 , $y_{i 1}=(1,0,0, \ldots, 0)$; for units in group $2, y_{i 1}=(0,1,0, \ldots, 0)$; for units in group $K$, $y_{i 1}=(0,0, \ldots, 0,1)$; and for units in group $K+1, y_{i 1}=(0,0, \ldots, 0,0)$; group $K+1$ is often called the reference group.

With these definitions, $\hat{\mu}_{1}$ is a vector consisting of the proportions of the $n$ sampled units in each of the first $K$ groups, $\mu_{1}$ is the corresponding vector of expected proportions, and aforementioned condition 2 is satisfied. Condition 1 is equivalent to assuming that all values of $y_{i 2}$ in group $k$ are normal with the same group mean and same variance $\sigma_{22 \cdot 1}$.

By the properties of dummy variable regression, the predicted value of $y_{i 2}$ for a unit in group $k$ is the mean of the observed values of $y_{i 2}$ in group $k$. Thus, the ML estimate corresponds to imputing the group means for missing values of $y_{i 2}$, a form of mean imputation that we discussed when considering nonresponse in sample surveys in Chapter 4.

### 7.4.2 The Factored Likelihood for a General Monotone Pattern

The methods described in Sections 7.2 and 7.3 can be readily generalized to the monotone pattern of data in Figure 1.1c, where for unit $i, y_{i j}$ is recorded if $y_{i, j+1}$ is recorded $(j=1, \ldots, J-1)$, so that $Y_{1}$ is more observed than $Y_{2}$, which
is more observed than $Y_{3}$, and so on (Rubin 1974). We confine attention to ML estimation. Precision of estimation and Bayesian inference can be addressed using straightforward extensions of the methods of Sections 7.2.2 and 7.3.

The appropriate factored likelihood for this pattern is

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \prod_{i=1}^{n} f_{Y}\left(y_{i 1}, \ldots, y_{i J} \mid \phi\right) \\
& \quad=\prod_{i=1}^{n} f_{1}\left(y_{i 1} \mid \phi_{1}\right) \prod_{i=1}^{r_{2}} f_{2}\left(y_{i 2} \mid y_{i 1}, \phi_{2}\right) \cdots \prod_{i=1}^{r_{J}} f_{J}\left(y_{i J} \mid y_{i 1}, \ldots, y_{i J-1}, \phi_{J}\right),
\end{aligned}
$$

where for $j=1, \ldots, J, f_{j}\left(y_{i j} \mid y_{i 1}, \ldots, y_{i, j-1}, \phi_{j}\right)$ is the conditional distribution of $y_{i j}$ given $y_{i 1}, \ldots, y_{i, j-1}$, indexed by the parameter $\phi_{j}$. If $\left(y_{i 1}, \ldots, y_{i j}\right)$ follows a multivariate normal distribution, then $f_{j}\left(y_{i j} \mid y_{i 1}, \ldots, y_{i, j-1}, \phi_{j}\right)$ is a normal distribution with mean linear in $y_{i 1}, \ldots, y_{i, j-1}$ and with constant variance. With the usual unrestricted natural parameter space of $\phi$, the $\phi_{j}$ are distinct, and so ML estimates of $\phi_{j}$ are obtained by regressing the $y_{i j}$ on the $y_{i 1}, \ldots, y_{i, j-1}$, using the set of units for which $y_{i 1}, \ldots, y_{i j}$ are all observed.

Example 7.5 Multivariate Normal Monotone Data. Marini et al. (1980) provide a numerical illustration of ML estimation for monotone data with $J>2$ patterns, for a panel study with 4352 units. The data pattern, given in Table 1.1, is not monotone, but as noted in Chapter 1, a monotone pattern can be achieved by discarding some data, in particular, those superscripted by the letter $b$ in the table. The resulting pattern is monotone as in Figure 1.1c with $J=4$. Assuming normality, ML estimates of the mean and covariance matrix of the variables can be found by the following procedure:

1. Calculate the mean vector and covariance matrix for the fully observed block 1 variables, from all the units.
2. Calculate the multivariate linear regression of the next most observed variables, block 2 variables on block 1 variables, from units with both block 1 and block 2 variables recorded.
3. Calculate the multivariate linear regression of block 3 variables on block 1 and 2 variables, from units with block $1-3$ variables recorded.
4. Calculate the multivariate linear regression of block 4 variables on block 1-3 variables from units with all variables recorded.

ML estimates of the means and covariance matrix of all the variables can be obtained as functions of the parameter estimates in $1-4$. The computational details, involving the powerful sweep operator, are discussed in the next section. Results are shown in Table 7.3.

The first column of Table 7.3 gives a description of the variables. The next two columns give ML estimates $\hat{\mu}_{\mathrm{ML}}$ of the means and $\hat{\sigma}_{\mathrm{ML}}$ of the standard

The next two columns display estimates ( $\hat{\mu}_{\mathrm{A}}, \hat{\sigma}_{\mathrm{A}}$ ) from the available cases method (that is, the sample means and standard deviations using all the units available for each variable, as in Section 3.4). The two columns after these estimates display the magnitude of differences between the ML and available-case estimates, measured in percent standard deviations estimated by ML. Even though these estimates of marginal parameters are quite close to the ML estimates, the available-case method is not generally recommended, as discussed in Chapter 3. Finally, the last four columns of the table present and compare with ML the estimates ( $\hat{\mu}_{\mathrm{CC}}, \hat{\sigma}_{\mathrm{CC}}$ ) based only on the 1594 complete units, the complete-case method discussed in Chapter 3. Estimates of means from this procedure sometimes differ markedly from the ML estimates. For example, the estimate for grade-point average is 0.35 of a standard deviation higher than the ML estimate, indicating that students lost to follow-up appear to have lower scores than average.

### 7.4.3 ML Computation for Monotone Normal Data via the Sweep Operator

We now review the use of the sweep operator (Beaton 1964) in linear regression with complete units and show how this operator provides a simple and convenient way to perform the ML calculations for incomplete normal data. The version of sweep we describe is not exactly the one originally defined in Beaton (1964); rather, it is the one defined by Dempster (1969); another accessible reference is Goodnight (1979). The sweep operator will also be useful in Chapter 9 when we consider ML estimation for normal data with a general pattern of missingness.

The sweep operator is defined for symmetric matrices as follows. The $p \times p$ symmetric matrix $G$ is said to be swept on row and column $k$ if it is replaced by another symmetric $p \times p$ matrix $H$ with elements defined as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{k k} & =-1 / g_{k k}, \\
h_{j k} & =h_{k j}=g_{j k} / g_{k k}, \quad j \neq k, \\
h_{j l} & =g_{j l}-g_{j k} g_{k l} / g_{k k}, \quad j \neq k, \quad l \neq k
\end{aligned}
$$

To illustrate (7.19), consider the $3 \times 3$ case:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G & =\left[\begin{array}{lll}
g_{11} & g_{12} & g_{13} \\
g_{12} & g_{22} & g_{23} \\
g_{13} & g_{23} & g_{33}
\end{array}\right], \\
H & =\operatorname{SWP}[1] G=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
-1 / g_{11} & g_{12} / g_{11} & g_{13} / g_{11} \\
g_{12} / g_{11} & g_{22}-g_{12}^{2} / g_{11} & g_{23}-g_{13} g_{12} / g_{11} \\
g_{13} / g_{11} & g_{23}-g_{13} g_{12} / g_{11} & g_{33}-g_{13}^{2} / g_{11}
\end{array}\right] .
\end{aligned}
$$

We use the notation SWP $[k] G$ to denote the matrix $H$ defined by (7.19). Also, the result of successively applying the operations SWP $\left[k_{1}\right], \mathrm{SWP}\left[k_{2}\right], \ldots$, $\operatorname{SWP}\left[k_{t}\right]$ to the matrix $G$ will be denoted by $\operatorname{SWP}\left[k_{1}, k_{2}, \ldots, k_{t}\right] G$. In actual computations, the sweep operation is efficiently achieved by first replacing $g_{k k}$ by $h_{k k}=-1 / g_{k k}$, then replacing the remaining elements $g_{j k}$ and $g_{k l}$ in row and column $k$ by $h_{j k}=h_{k j}=-g_{j k} h_{k k}$, and finally replacing elements $g_{j l}$ that are neither in row $k$ nor in column $k$ by $h_{j l}=g_{j l}-h_{j k} g_{k l}$. Storage space can be saved by storing the distinct elements of the symmetric $p \times p$ matrices in vectors of length $p(p+1) / 2$, so that for $k \leq j$, the $(j, k)$ th element of the matrix is stored as the $(j(j-1) / 2+k)$ th element of the vector.

Some algebra shows that the sweep operator is commutative, that is

$$
\mathrm{SWP}[j, k] G=\mathrm{SWP}[k, j] G .
$$

It follows more generally that

$$
\operatorname{SWP}\left[j_{1}, \ldots, j_{t}\right] G=\operatorname{SWP}\left[k_{1}, \ldots, k_{t}\right] G,
$$

where $j_{1}, \ldots, j_{t}$ is any permutation of $k_{1}, \ldots, k_{t}$. That is, the order in which a set of sweeps is carried out does not affect the final answer algebraically, although some orders may be computationally more accurate than others.

The sweep operator is closely related to linear regression. For example, suppose that $G$ is a ( $2 \times 2$ ) covariance matrix of two variables $Y_{1}$ and $Y_{2}$, and let $H=$ SWP[1]G. Then $h_{12}$ is the coefficient of $Y_{1}$ from the regression of $Y_{2}$ on $Y_{1}$, and $h_{22}$ is the residual variance of $Y_{2}$ given $Y_{1}$. Furthermore, when $G$ is a sample covariance matrix from $n$ independent units, $-h_{11} h_{22} / n$ is the estimated sampling variance of the sample regression coefficient, $h_{12}$.

More generally, suppose we have a sample of $n$ units on $K$ variables $Y_{1}, \ldots, Y_{K}$. Let $G$ denote the $(K+1) \times(K+1)$ matrix

$$
G=\left[\begin{array}{cccccc}
1 & \bar{y}_{1} & \cdots & \bar{y}_{j} & \cdots & \bar{y}_{K} \\
\bar{y}_{1} & n^{-1} \sum y_{1}^{2} & & & \cdots & n^{-1} \sum y_{K} y_{1} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & & & \vdots \\
\bar{y}_{k} & & & n^{-1} \sum y_{j} y_{k} & & \\
& & & & \ddots & \\
\bar{y}_{K} & n^{-1} \sum y_{1} y_{K} & & & \cdots & n^{-1} \sum y_{K}^{2}
\end{array}\right],
$$

where $\bar{y}_{1}, \ldots, \bar{y}_{K}$ are the sample means, and summations are over the $n$ units. For convenience, we index the rows and columns from 0 to $K$, so that row and column $j$ corresponds to variable $Y_{j}$. Sweeping on row and column 0 yields

$$
\operatorname{SWP}[0] G=\left[\begin{array}{cccccc}
-1 & \bar{y}_{1} & \cdots & \bar{y}_{j} & \cdots & \bar{y}_{K} \\
\bar{y}_{1} & \hat{\sigma}_{11} & & & \cdots & \hat{\sigma}_{K 1} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & & & \vdots \\
\bar{y}_{k} & & & \hat{\sigma}_{j k} & & \\
& & & & \ddots & \\
\bar{y}_{K} & \hat{\sigma}_{1 K} & & & \cdots & \hat{\sigma}_{K K}
\end{array}\right],
$$

where $\hat{\sigma}_{j k}$ is the sample covariance of $Y_{j}$ and $Y_{k}$, with denominator $n$ rather than $(n-1)$. This operation corresponds to correcting the scaled cross-products matrix of $Y_{1}, \ldots, Y_{K}, G$, for the means of $Y_{1}, \ldots, Y_{K}$ to create the covariance matrix. In terms of regression, the means in the first row and column of SWP [0] $G$ are coefficients from the regression of $Y_{1}, \ldots, Y_{K}$ on the constant term $Y_{0} \equiv 1$, and the corrected, scaled cross-products matrix $\left\{\hat{\sigma}_{j k}\right\}$ is the residual covariance matrix (with divisor $n$ rather than $n-1$ ) from this regression. Thus, we also call this process sweeping on the constant term. We call (7.20) the augmented covariance matrix of the variables $Y_{1}, \ldots, Y_{K}$.

Sweeping (7.20) on row and column 1, corresponding to $Y_{1}$, yields the symmetric matrix

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{SWP}[0,1] G & =\left[\begin{array}{ccccc}
-\left(1+\bar{y}_{1} / \hat{\sigma}_{11}\right) & \bar{y}_{11} / \hat{\sigma}_{11} & \bar{y}_{2}-\left(\hat{\sigma}_{12} / \hat{\sigma}_{11}\right) \bar{y}_{1} & \cdots & \bar{y}_{K}-\left(\hat{\sigma}_{1 K} / \hat{\sigma}_{11}\right) \bar{y}_{1} \\
& -1 / \hat{\sigma}_{11} & \hat{\sigma}_{12} / \hat{\sigma}_{11} & \cdots & \hat{\sigma}_{1 K} / \hat{\sigma}_{11} \\
& & \hat{\sigma}_{22}-\hat{\sigma}_{12}^{2} / \hat{\sigma}_{11} & \cdots & \hat{\sigma}_{2 K}-\hat{\sigma}_{1 K} \hat{\sigma}_{12} / \hat{\sigma}_{11} \\
& & & & \vdots \\
\bar{y}_{K}-\left(\hat{\sigma}_{1 K} / \hat{\sigma}_{11}\right) \bar{y}_{1} & & & & \hat{\sigma}_{K K}-\hat{\sigma}_{1 K}^{2} / \hat{\sigma}_{11}
\end{array}\right] \\
& =\left[\begin{array}{cc}
-A & B \\
B^{\mathrm{T}} & C
\end{array}\right],
\end{aligned}
$$

say, where $A$ is ( $2 \times 2$ ), $B$ is $2 \times(K-1)$, and $C$ is $(K-1) \times(K-1)$. This matrix yields results for the (multivariate) regression of $Y_{2}, \ldots, Y_{K}$ on $Y_{1}$. In particular, the $j$ th column of $B$ gives the intercept and slope for the regression of $Y_{j+1}$ on $Y_{1}$ for $j=1, \ldots, K-1$. The matrix $C$ gives the residual covariance matrix of $Y_{2}, \ldots, Y_{K}$ given $Y_{1}$. Finally, the elements of $A$, when multiplied by the appropriate residual variance or covariance in $C$ and divided by $n$, yield the asymptotic sampling variances and covariances of the estimated regression coefficients in $B$.

Sweeping the constant term and the first $q$ elements yields results for the multivariate regression of $Y_{q+1}, \ldots, Y_{K}$ on $Y_{1}, \ldots, Y_{q}$. Specifically, letting

$$
\operatorname{SWP}[0,1, \ldots, q] G=\left[\begin{array}{cc}
-D & E \\
E^{\mathrm{T}} & F
\end{array}\right]
$$



where $D$ is $(q+1) \times(q+1), E$ is $(q+1) \times(K-q)$, and $F$ is $(K-q) \times(K-q)$, the $j$ th column of $E$ gives the least, squares intercept and slopes of the regression of $Y_{j+q}$ on $Y_{1}, \ldots, Y_{q}$, for $j=1,2, \ldots, K-q$; the matrix $F$ is the residual covariance matrix of $Y_{q+1}, \ldots, Y_{K}$; and the elements of $D$ can be used, as previously mentioned, to give the asymptotic sampling variances and covariances of the estimated regression coefficients in $E$.

In summary, ML estimates for the multivariate linear regression of $Y_{q+1}, \ldots, Y_{K}$ on $Y_{1}, \ldots, Y_{q}$ can be found by sweeping the rows and columns corresponding to the constant term and the predictor variables $Y_{1}, \ldots, Y_{q}$ from the scaled cross-products matrix $G$.

The operation of sweeping on a variable in effect turns that variable from an outcome (or dependent) variable into a predictor (or independent) variable. There is also an operator inverse to sweep that turns predictor variables into outcome variables. This operator is called reverse sweep (RSW) and is defined by

$$
H=\operatorname{RSW}[k] G,
$$

where

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{k k} & =-1 / g_{k k}, \\
h_{j k} & =h_{k j}=-g_{j k} / g_{k k}, \quad j \neq k \\
h_{j l} & =g_{j l}-g_{j k} g_{k l} / g_{k k}, \quad j \neq k, \quad l \neq k
\end{aligned}
$$

It is readily verified that reverse sweep is also commutative and is the inverse operator to sweep; that is

$$
(\operatorname{RSW}[k])(\operatorname{SWP}[k]) G=(\operatorname{SWP}[k])(\operatorname{RSW}[k]) G=G .
$$

Example 7.6 Bivariate Normal Monotone Data (Example 7.1 Continued). Various parameterizations of the bivariate normal distribution are easily related using the sweep and reverse sweep operators. Thus, the parameters $\theta$ and $\phi$ of Example 7.1 and the relationships in (7.4) and (7.5) can be compactly expressed using the SWP[ ] and RSW[ ] notation. Also, numerical values of standard functions of ML estimates can be simply computed using these operators.

Suppose we arrange $\theta=\left(\mu_{1}, \mu_{2}, \sigma_{11}, \sigma_{12}, \sigma_{22}\right)^{\mathrm{T}}$ in the following ( $3 \times 3$ ) symmetric matrix, which is the population analog of (7.20):

$$
\theta^{*}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
-1 & \mu_{1} & \mu_{2} \\
\mu_{1} & \sigma_{11} & \sigma_{12} \\
\mu_{2} & \sigma_{12} & \sigma_{22}
\end{array}\right]
$$

The matrix $\theta^{*}$ represents the parameters of the bivariate normal with the constant swept. If $\theta^{*}$ is swept on row and column 1 , then we obtain from (7.19)

$$
\operatorname{SWP}[1] \theta^{*}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
-\left(1+\mu_{1}^{2} / \sigma_{11}\right) & \mu_{1} / \sigma_{11} & \mu_{2}-\mu_{1} \sigma_{12} / \sigma_{11} \\
\mu_{1} / \sigma_{11} & -\sigma_{11}^{-1} & \sigma_{12} / \sigma_{11} \\
\mu_{2}-\mu_{1} \sigma_{12} / \sigma_{11} & \sigma_{12} / \sigma_{11} & \sigma_{22}-\sigma_{12}^{2} / \sigma_{11}
\end{array}\right]
$$

An examination of (7.4) reveals that row (or column) 2 of SWP $[1] \theta^{*}$ provides the intercept, $\mu_{2}-\mu_{1} \sigma_{12} / \sigma_{11}$; the slope of the regression of $Y_{2}$ on $Y_{1}, \sigma_{12} / \sigma_{11}$; and the residual variance, $\sigma_{22 \cdot 1}=\sigma_{22}-\sigma_{12}^{2} / \sigma_{11}$. Also, although not in a particularly familiar form, the $2 \times 2$ submatrix formed by the first two rows and columns provides the parameters of the marginal distribution of $Y_{1}$. To see this, write

$$
\phi^{*}=\operatorname{SWP}[1] \theta^{*}=\left[\begin{array}{lcc}
\operatorname{SWP}[1] & {\left[\begin{array}{cc}
-1 & \mu_{1} \\
\mu_{1} & \sigma_{11}
\end{array}\right]} & \beta_{20 \cdot 1} \\
& \beta_{20 \cdot 1 \cdot 1} & \beta_{21 \cdot 1} \\
& \sigma_{22 \cdot 1}
\end{array}\right],
$$

where $\phi^{*}$ is a slightly modified version of $\phi=\left(\mu_{1}, \sigma_{11}, \beta_{20 \cdot 1}, \beta_{21 \cdot 1}, \sigma_{22 \cdot 1}\right)^{\mathrm{T}}$, displayed as a matrix. By Property 6.1, a similar expression relates the ML estimates of $\theta$ to the ML estimates of $\phi$ :

$$
\hat{\phi}^{*}=\operatorname{SWP}[1] \hat{\theta}^{*}=\left[\begin{array}{lcc}
\mathrm{SWP}[1] & {\left[\begin{array}{cc}
-1 & \hat{\mu}_{1} \\
\hat{\mu}_{1} & \hat{\sigma}_{11}
\end{array}\right]} & \hat{\beta}_{20 \cdot 1} \\
& \hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1} \\
& \hat{\beta}_{20 \cdot 1} & \hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1}
\end{array}\right.
$$

Applying the RSW[1] operator to both sides yields

$$
\hat{\theta}^{*}=\operatorname{RSW}[1]\left[\begin{array}{lcc}
\mathrm{SWP}[1] & {\left[\begin{array}{cc}
-1 & \hat{\mu}_{1} \\
\hat{\mu}_{1} & \hat{\sigma}_{11}
\end{array}\right]} & \hat{\beta}_{20 \cdot 1} \\
& \hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1} \\
& \hat{\beta}_{20 \cdot 1} & \hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1}
\end{array}\right.
$$

Expression (7.23) defines the transformation from $\hat{\phi}$ to $\hat{\theta}$ in terms of the sweep and reverse sweep operators and thus shows how these operators can be used to compute $\hat{\theta}$ from $\hat{\phi}$.

Example 7.7 Multivariate Normal Monotone Data (Example 7.5 Continued). We now extend Example 7.6 to show how the sweep and reverse sweep operators can be applied to find ML estimates of the mean and covariance matrix of a multivariate normal distribution from data with a monotone pattern. We assume that the data have the monotone pattern of Figure 1.1c, after suitable
arrangement of the variables. Also for simplicity, we consider the case with $J=3$ blocks of variables. The extension to more than three blocks of variables is immediate.

Step 1: Find the ML estimates $\hat{\mu}_{1}$ and $\widehat{\Sigma}_{11}$ of the mean $\mu_{1}$ and covariance matrix $\Sigma_{11}$ of the first block of variables, which are completely observed. These are simply the sample mean and sample covariance matrix of $Y_{1}$ based on all the units.
Step 2: Find the ML estimates $\hat{\beta}_{20 \cdot 1}, \hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1}$, and $\widehat{\Sigma}_{22 \cdot 1}$ of the intercepts, regression coefficients, and residual covariance matrix for the regression of $Y_{2}$ on $Y_{1}$. These can be found by sweeping the variables $Y_{1}$ out of the augmented covariance matrix of $Y_{1}$ and $Y_{2}$ based on the units with $Y_{1}$ and $Y_{2}$ both observed.
Step 3: Find the ML estimates $\hat{\beta}_{30 \cdot 12}, \hat{\beta}_{31 \cdot 12}, \hat{\beta}_{32 \cdot 12}$, and $\widehat{\Sigma}_{33 \cdot 12}$ of the intercepts, regression coefficients, and residual covariance matrix for the regression of $Y_{3}$ on $Y_{1}$ and $Y_{2}$. These can be found by sweeping the variables $Y_{1}$ and $Y_{2}$ out of the augmented covariance matrix of $Y_{1}, Y_{2}$, and $Y_{3}$ based on the units with $Y_{1}, Y_{2}$, and $Y_{3}$ observed.
Step 4: Calculate the matrix

$$
A=\operatorname{SWP}[1]\left[\begin{array}{cc}
-1 & \hat{\mu}_{1}^{\mathrm{T}} \\
\hat{\mu}_{1} & \widehat{\Sigma}_{11}
\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll}
a_{11} & a_{12} \\
a_{12} & A_{22}
\end{array}\right],
$$

where SWP [1] is shorthand for sweeping on the set of variables $Y_{1}$.
Step 5: Calculate the matrix

$$
B=\operatorname{SWP}[2]\left[\begin{array}{lll}
a_{11} & a_{12} & \hat{\beta}_{20 \cdot 1}^{\mathrm{T}} \\
a_{21} & A_{22} & \hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1}^{\mathrm{T}} \\
\hat{\beta}_{20 \cdot 1} & \hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1} & \hat{\Sigma}_{22 \cdot 1}
\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll}
c_{11} & c_{12} & c_{13} \\
c_{21} & c_{22} & c_{23} \\
c_{31} & c_{32} & c_{33}
\end{array}\right],
$$

where SWP[2] is shorthand for sweeping on the set of variables $Y_{2}$.
Step 6: Finally, the ML estimate of the augmented covariance matrix of $Y_{1}, Y_{2}$, and $Y_{3}$ is given by

$$
\left[\begin{array}{cc}
-1 & \hat{\mu}^{\mathrm{T}} \\
\hat{\mu} & \widehat{\Sigma}
\end{array}\right]=\operatorname{RSW}[1,2]\left[\begin{array}{cccc}
c_{11} & c_{12} & c_{13} & \hat{\beta}_{20 \cdot 1}^{\mathrm{T}} \\
c_{21} & c_{22} & c_{23} & \hat{\beta}_{31 \cdot 12}^{\mathrm{T}} \\
c_{31} & c_{32} & c_{33} & \hat{\beta}_{32 \cdot 12}^{\mathrm{T}} \\
\hat{\beta}_{20 \cdot 1} & \hat{\beta}_{31 \cdot 12} & \hat{\beta}_{32 \cdot 12} & \hat{\Sigma}_{33 \cdot 12}
\end{array}\right] \text {. }
$$

This matrix contains the ML estimates of the mean and covariance matrix of $Y_{1}, Y_{2}$, and $Y_{3}$, as indicated.

Steps 4-6 can be represented concisely by the equation

$$
\left[\begin{array}{cc}
-1 & \hat{\mu}^{\mathrm{T}} \\
\hat{\mu} & \hat{\Sigma}
\end{array}\right]=\operatorname{RSW}[1,2]\left[\operatorname{SWP}[2]\left[\begin{array}{lllll}
\operatorname{SWP}[1] & {\left[\begin{array}{cc}
-1 & \hat{\mu}_{1}^{T} \\
& \hat{\mu}_{1} \\
\hat{\Sigma}_{11}
\end{array}\right]} & \hat{\beta}_{20 \cdot 1}^{\mathrm{T}} & \hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1}^{\mathrm{T}} \\
& \hat{\beta}_{20 \cdot 1} & \hat{\beta}_{21 \cdot 1} & \hat{\Sigma}_{22 \cdot 1}
\end{array}\right] \hat{\beta}_{31 \cdot 12}^{\mathrm{T}},\right.
$$

with obvious generalizations to more than three blocks of variables. This equation defines the transformation from $\hat{\phi}$ to $\hat{\theta}$ for this situation.

Estimates of the sampling precision of the ML estimates based on the asymptotic covariance matrix are not as easily accomplished by such operations. However, a simple alternative approach is to adopt a Bayesian perspective and estimate the precision using the posterior variance, as introduced in Section 7.3 for bivariate normal monotone data. We discuss this approach in Section 7.4.4.

Example 7.8 A Numerical Example. Rubin (1976c) presents the previously described calculations for the data in Table 7.4, described in Draper and

| Unit | Variables |  |  |  |  |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
|  | $X_{1}$ | $X_{2}$ | $X_{3}$ | $X_{4}$ | $Y=X_{5}$ |
| 1 | 7 | 26 | 6 | 60 | 78.5 |
| 2 | 1 | 29 | 15 | 52 | 74.3 |
| 3 | 11 | 56 | 8 | 20 | 104.3 |
| 4 | 11 | 31 | 8 | 47 | 87.6 |
| 5 | 7 | 52 | 6 | 33 | 95.9 |
| 6 | 11 | 55 | 9 | 22 | 109.2 |

| 8 | 1 | 31 | 22 | (44) | 72.5 |
| 9 | 2 | 54 | 18 | (22) | 93.1 |
| 10 | (21) | (47) | 4 | (26) | 115.9 |
| 11 | (1) | (40) | 23 | (34) | 83.8 |
| 12 | (11) | (66) | 9 | (12) | 113.3 |
| 13 | (10) | (68) | 8 | (12) | 109.4 |


[^0]Smith (1981). The original labeling of the variables is $X_{1}, \ldots, X_{5}$. The data have the pattern of Figure 1.1c with $J=3$ and $Y_{1}=\left(X_{3}, X_{5}\right), Y_{2}=\left(X_{1}, X_{2}\right)$, and $Y_{3}=X_{4}$. We first apply the method of Example 7.7 to yield ML estimates of the parameters.

Step 1 gives the ML estimates of the marginal distribution of ( $X_{3}, X_{5}$ ) as

$$
\begin{array}{ccc}
\hat{\mu}_{3}=11.769, & \hat{\mu}_{5}=95.423, & \hat{\sigma}_{33}=37.870 \\
\hat{\sigma}_{35}=-47.566, & \hat{\sigma}_{55}=208.905 &
\end{array}
$$

Step 2 gives ML estimates of the coefficients from the regression of $\left(X_{1}, X_{2}\right)$ on $\left(X_{3}, X_{5}\right)$, based on units 1-9:

$$
\begin{array}{ccc}
\hat{\beta}_{10 \cdot 35}=2.802, & \hat{\beta}_{13 \cdot 35}=-0.526, & \hat{\beta}_{15 \cdot 35}=0.105 \\
\hat{\beta}_{20 \cdot 35}=-74.938, & \hat{\beta}_{23 \cdot 35}=1.062, & \hat{\beta}_{25 \cdot 35}=1.178
\end{array}
$$

and the residual covariance matrix

$$
\widehat{\Sigma}_{12 \cdot 35}=\begin{gathered}
\\
X_{1} \\
X_{2}
\end{gathered} \begin{array}{cc}
X_{1} & X_{2} \\
{\left[\begin{array}{cc}
3.804 & -8.011 \\
-8.011 & 24.382
\end{array}\right] .}
\end{array}
$$

Step 3 gives ML estimates of the coefficients and residual variance for the regression of $X_{4}$ on the other variables, based on units 1-6:

$$
\begin{array}{lcc}
\hat{\beta}_{40 \cdot 1235}=85.753, & \hat{\beta}_{41 \cdot 1235}=-1.863, & \hat{\beta}_{42 \cdot 1235}=-1.324 \\
\hat{\beta}_{43 \cdot 1235}=-1.533, & \hat{\beta}_{45 \cdot 1235}=0.397, & \hat{\sigma}_{44 \cdot 1235}=0.046
\end{array}
$$

Steps 4-6 yield
$\left[\begin{array}{cc}-1 & \hat{\mu}^{\mathrm{T}} \\ \hat{\mu} & \hat{\Sigma}\end{array}\right]=\operatorname{RSW[1235]}\left[\operatorname{SWP[12]}\left[\begin{array}{l}\text { SWP[35] }\left[\begin{array}{crrrr}-1 & 11.769 & 95.423 \\ 11.769 & 37.870 & -47.566 \\ 95.423 & -47.566 & 208.905\end{array}\right] \\ \\ 2.802 \\ -0.526 \\ -74.938 \\ 1.062 \\ 85.753\end{array}-1.533 \quad \begin{array}{rrr}2.802 & -74.938 \\ -0.526 & 1.062 & 0.105 \\ 1.178 & 3.804 & -8.011 \\ -1.533 \\ 0.397 \\ -1.863 \\ 0.11 & 24.382\end{array}\right]\right.$

Calculating the right side and reordering the variables gives ML estimates

$$
\begin{gathered}
x_{1} \\
